We’re seeing great results using AI to update how we do compliance, a high stakes area of the company.

We rebuilt essentially every workflow, finding huge efficiency unlocks (e.g. 90% faster restriction resolution times). Humans still validate every outcome to maintain security and optimize models, but AI does most of the heavy lifting on repetitive work, freeing up human time for higher level decisions.

3. Anything invented after you’re thirty-five is against the natural order of things.
—Douglas Adams

tweets.text:
Several participants highlighted that it would likely be appropriate to lower the target range for the federal funds rate once there are clear indications that disinflation is firmly back on track or if solid signs emerge of greater weakness in the labor market. 

A majority of participants highlighted, however, that some policy firming would likely become appropriate if inflation were to continue to run persistently above 2 percent. 

To address this possibility, many participants indicated that they would have preferred removing the language from the postmeeting statement that suggested an easing bias regarding the likely direction of the Committee’s future interest rate decisions.

tweets.full_text:
Several participants highlighted that it would likely be appropriate to lower the target range for the federal funds rate once there are clear indications that disinflation is firmly back on track or if solid signs emerge of greater weakness in the labor market. 

A majority of participants highlighted, however, that some policy firming would likely become appropriate if inflation were to continue to run persistently above 2 percent. 

To address this possibility, many participants indicated that they would have preferred removing the language from the postmeeting statement that suggested an easing bias regarding the likely direction of the Committee’s future interest rate decisions.

tweets.text:
Food is set to become even more expensive:

World fertilizer prices have surged +44% since the start of the Iran War, to the highest since 2022.

This comes as ~33% of globally traded fertilizers pass through the Strait of Hormuz, which remains effectively closed.

This includes 23% of global ammonia, 34% of urea, the world's most widely used nitrogen fertilizer, and nearly 20% of global phosphate supply.

Furthermore, the Bloomberg Agriculture Subindex has increased ~9% since the Iran war.

The index tracks the futures prices of key agricultural commodities, including wheat, corn, soybeans, sugar, coffee, and cotton.

In the past, world fertilizer prices have acted as a leading indicator for agricultural output prices, as rising production costs eventually force farmers to reduce supply, pushing crop prices higher.

A new wave of global food inflation is imminent.

The SPR drain can't continue past July. Every day of a stalemate leads to the risk of energy and food security having some pretty nasty outcomes.

@K9Cognoscente The US will 100% shutdown the SPR drain in July at the latest. It’s not possible to continue past that at this pace. They have maybe 6-8 weeks left of runway. Delays imo work in Irans favor bc they are willing to be Martyrs and are fanatical. It doesn’t work in the US’s favor

Marc Andreessen says AI is teaching sand to think and it could be the most important technology in the history of humanity:

"Imagine a form of alchemy that turns sand into thought."

"Chips are made out of sand. They're made out of silicon, so they're literally made out of sand."

"We plug the chip into a data center, into power, we light it up, and we put AI on it, and all of a sudden it's thinking."

"We've turned sand into thought. And so it's possibly the most revolutionary technology in the history of the species."

"It's certainly on par with electricity and steam power. It's certainly more important than the internet."

@pmarca with @joerogan

tweets.text:
We shouldn't lose sight of  the behavioral angle:
The impressive robustness of the US consumer in the face of higher gas prices -- with regular back at $4.55 and diesel at $5.65 (AAA data below) -- is not just a function of financial buffers and other financial factors.
A behavioral aspect has also played a role: the belief that the price surge is both temporary and quickly reversible...and, therefore, doesn't warrant changing consumption habits.
This psychological resilience erodes over time, not just the longer prices remain high (and we are now nearing the end of the second month of this price shock), but also when prices bounce back up after a drop -- which is what has happened over the last week.
#economy #gas #inflation #growth #markets

tweets.text:
We’re excited to lead Exa’s Series C, to back their ambition of perfecting web search, and making it ready for the age of intelligent agents.

We’re entering a new era for search, built from the ground up around what AI can do.

The signal from the market was consistent. Exa excels with the hardest queries – the long tail of high alpha searches where traditional engines fail. What struck us most was the default behavior: developers and agents are reaching for Exa first.

Years before ChatGPT or the AI boom really started, @WilliamBryk and @jeffzwang were inspired by the transformer breakthrough and believed deeply that AI will fundamentally change the way we access information.

So they set out on the path to build the search engine for a future where agents become the primary consumers of the web.

We’re thrilled to partner with Will, Jeff, and the Exa team as they build the perfect search engine for agents and usher in a world of abundant information for all.

By @sarahdingwang, @JenniferHli, @steph_zhang, and @JasonSCui
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
We raised $250M in Series C funding at a $2.2B valuation, led by a16z.

Exa is a search lab organizing the web's data for agents. https://t.co/wB3IYAskc3

tweets.text:
You said:
"Food prices up 50%. 
Wages barely moved. 
Billionaire wealth exploded."

And said it was greed. It isn't, it is the cantillion effect.

Inflation is what is making people poorer. You can tax all the billionaires as much as you want, if you the state does not stop inflation or at least allow an environment for growth to outpace inflation and wages keep up, things will get much worse.

The economy is complex and sensitive. Poor choices kill it, just like the physics of an rocket, you don't give that to amateurs else the rocket will blow up.

Highlights so far:

Stake running BAM roughly doubled from ~14% to ~28% in Q1 and is now around 31–32%, with validator count up ~50%.

FireBAM is nearly ready for mainnet.

Feeling positive on the DAO choosing reinvestment over immediate value accrual over the past six months, arguing returns on growth are currently high.

BAM stake is at all-time highs, around 32% + a hint that JIP may adjust Q3 incentive step-downs into more of a cliff ending in Q3.

tweets.full_text:
Highlights so far:

Stake running BAM roughly doubled from ~14% to ~28% in Q1 and is now around 31–32%, with validator count up ~50%.

FireBAM is nearly ready for mainnet.

Feeling positive on the DAO choosing reinvestment over immediate value accrual over the past six months, arguing returns on growth are currently high.

BAM stake is at all-time highs, around 32% + a hint that JIP may adjust Q3 incentive step-downs into more of a cliff ending in Q3.

tweets.text:
Aave is moving from a lending protocol to a full-stack financial platform.

The protocol has a structural problem at scale. USDC borrowers in Prime pay 3.7% while Core USDC borrowers pay 3.4%. The two instances are completely isolated.

Prime has roughly $532M in market size and Core has $35B, and the rates drift away from what an efficient market would produce.

Aave v4 is built to fix this. Its Hub & Spoke architecture turns instances like Prime and Core into hubs that can share liquidity with each other through approved credit lines. Prime can then borrow from Core and the rates converge.

v4 extends Aave's network effects across every current and future instance, but it does not rebuild the core trust model. Governance still authorizes every spoke and grants every credit line.

The bigger question is whether Aave can build the consumer products on top of it at scale.

tweets.text: That's a rather generous reading: in Oct 2025, UK gov announced it would ban all imports of refined products made with Russian oil in third countries. Typically, London moved on oil sanctions in unison with Brussels. But EU went ahead with the ban in Jan 2026 and London procrastinated. Then, yesterday, to the surprise of every European ally, it introduced the restrictions, but watered down from its Oct announcement, allowing the importation of diesel and jet fuel (which are the two fuels that matter, so effectively, the new measure is completely toothless).

tweets.text:
The Fed balance sheet vs S&P 500 chart is one of the most cited charts in macro. It is also misleading.

The relationship only looks clean in raw levels. Convert to year-over-year growth and it weakens. The sign actually flips on global monetary base growth across the major central banks, where growth correlates negatively with forward equity returns.

The simple level overlay is not evidence of a leading indicator. It is two heavily trending series plotted together. Anything that goes up over time will correlate with anything else that goes up over time.

The same problem shows up when market liquidity indices are treated as leading indicators. Measures like the Bloomberg US Government Securities Liquidity Index are cited to explain selloffs. A correlation that peaks at zero months is coincident by definition. They tell you markets are stressed while markets are stressed.

The fix is to separate measures that lead from measures that confirm. Central bank balance sheets don't directly capture private-sector credit creation, leverage, or risk appetite. Those are what matter and they don't show up cleanly on the Fed's balance sheet.

A framework that tracks the market instead of leading it will get wrong-footed at every turning point.

tweets.text:
Gavin on why Elon's new chip fab, Terafab, will be successful:

"One, they have a partnership with Intel, which is very important. They're getting access to 50 years of institutional knowledge.

The A teams at the semi cap equipment companies will be working with them. ASML, KLA, Lam Research, Applied Materials. 

One big reason Taiwan Semi caught up is those companies wanted them to catch up. They don't like having a monopsony.

And Elon is a living deity in China, Taiwan, South Korea, and Japan. 

He's going to recruit the best people because the best engineers want to work for him, especially in hardware engineering.

Then next to Terafab, there'll be a Taiwan Town. 'These are your favorite restaurants? I'm going to move them and their whole staff from Taiwan to Texas.' Then we'll have Japan Town. Then Korea Town. 

That's just not the way the people who run Intel and Samsung think.

So he's going to have the best talent, the A teams at the wafer fab equipment companies and he has Intel.

And it's different enough that it will not alienate Taiwan Semi."

tweets.text:
Coinbase and Circle’s Hyperliquid deal is more than PR. USDC has post-GENIUS momentum, but supply share has barely moved.

Hyperliquid gives it the missing piece: a globally accessible trading venue where stablecoin distribution can actually compound.

In April 2025, USDT held 67% of stablecoin supply while USDC held 27.6%. A year later, USDT sits at 67.3% and USDC at 28.1%. USDC transaction volume is accelerating, but the structural picture is basically unchanged

That is the problem.

USDC is strongest in the U.S., but that is exactly where competition is now concentrating. Stripe has Tempo. Major financial institutions are launching their own GENIUS-compliant stables. Tether even introduced USAT to attack USDC inside the U.S. regulatory perimeter.

Abroad, USDT is still the default dollar for saving, investing, and trading.
So Coinbase and Circle need distribution, and perpetuals are the obvious venue.

Stablecoins are the quote asset perps are built around. On Binance, that means USDT. Anyone trading Binance’s biggest markets is mostly trading against USDT, which reinforces USDT’s supply, liquidity, deposits, withdrawals, and onchain activity.

Hyperliquid gives USDC a way to fight that dynamic.

> Hyperliquid holds 30% of onchain perp market share

> It commands 46% of onchain open interest

> It runs at ~50% of Bybit’s volume, ~30% of OKX’s, ~79% of Coinbase International’s, and ~13% of Binance’s

Still smaller than Binance, but clearly moving in one direction.

Sure, Coinbase could try to build this distribution itself, but they wouldn't get far. Regulation limits its reach. Coinbase is available in roughly 100 countries, while Binance reaches around 180. Hyperliquid’s more permissive structure gives it global reach Coinbase cannot easily replicate.

So Coinbase and Circle let Hyperliquid carry the reach while USDC rides underneath.

Tether sees the same battlefield. After Drift’s exploit, it committed up to $147.5M to help relaunch the Solana perp DEX with USDT at the center.
Both sides understand the same thing: perpetuals are where stablecoin distribution gets locked in.

David Christopher’s read: Hyperliquid gives USDC a chance to become the base currency for the most important onchain trading venue in crypto, in one of the fastest-growing categories in the entire market.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: https://t.co/r9sMnsxpCS

So Coinbase and Circle let Hyperliquid carry the reach while USDC rides underneath.

Tether sees the same battlefield. After Drift’s exploit, it committed up to $147.5M to help relaunch the Solana perp DEX with USDT at the center.
Both sides understand the same thing: perpetuals are where stablecoin distribution gets locked in.

David Christopher’s read: Hyperliquid gives USDC a chance to become the base currency for the most important onchain trading venue in crypto, in one of the fastest-growing categories in the entire market.

tweets.text:
Gavin Baker: "I've been optimistic that the fundamental shortage of wafers, which is really controlled by Taiwan Semi, will prevent a bubble."

"If Taiwan Semi did what Jensen wanted, Nvidia could sell $2 trillion of GPUs in 2026 or 2027. 

But there is a limit where consumers would consume so much that you'd probably be in an overbuild.

And you are starting to see companies go to Intel and Samsung.  

A lot of this may come down to the degree to which Taiwan Semi can maintain a lead over Intel and Samsung and the pace at which they expand capacity.

If I were to watch one thing to understand whether there's a bubble, it's Taiwan Semi's capacity decisions. 

There's a Goldilocks zone where they expand enough to make it hard for Intel or Samsung to emerge as a second source, but they also keep the fundamental constraint on wafers that helps us avoid a bubble."

tweets.text:
Institutions have already bought Bitcoin.

ETFs, public companies, and treasuries solved the exposure problem.

The next question is what do they do with that $BTC once it’s sitting on the balance sheet?

That’s where Stacks’ Bitcoin staking design gets interesting.

Why? Because it's built around BTC staying on Bitcoin L1, under the holder’s own keys, while earning BTC-denominated yield.

STX acts as the capacity asset in the system, with a proposed 3% BTC APY, and early exit built into the design.

That matters because staking could become the entry point into the stacks ecosystem. 

Once BTC capital starts earning natively, the app layer around it gets more useful too:

• @ZestProtocol for BTC lending.
• @GraniteBTC for BTC-backed borrowing.
• @bitflow for liquidity.
• @HermeticaFi for BTC yield products.
• $sBTC and $USDCx as the core rails.

So institutions are finally getting a cleaner path from passive BTC exposure into Bitcoin-native finance.

Pleased to partner with @Stacks on this one.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
How Bitcoin Staking works in four steps:

1. Acquire STX
2. Lock BTC + STX
3. Earn BTC yield every cycle
4. Unlock and repeat https://t.co/FxwzGMCk2Z

tweets.text:
the idea with L2s wasn’t completely idiotic when you think about it in its original context, idealistic OGs believed that the assets themselves like BTC and ETH would reach mainstream adoption as the new global money, and as such people will want to use tech that preserves BTC’s and ETH’s “decentralized” properties as assets while transacting on faster and cheaper rails

that never happened though, when people use crypto app they basically only use them with USDC. the native assets like BTC ETH and SOL (and HYPE) are for holding, not for using. USDC is by definition centralized so no one in their right mind should insist on using a “decentralized” L2 just to transact in a centralized asset. so even the idealistic vision that OGs had simply doesn’t fit the reality of which assets people want to use in high velocity

the idea with L2s wasn’t completely idiotic when you think about it in its original context, idealistic OGs believed that the assets themselves like BTC and ETH would reach mainstream adoption as the new global money, and as such people will want to use tech that preserves BTC’s and ETH’s “decentralized” properties as assets while transacting on faster and cheaper rails

that never happened though, when people use crypto app they basically only use them with USDC. the native assets like BTC ETH and SOL (and HYPE) are for holding, not for using. USDC is by definition centralized so no one in their right mind should insist on using a “decentralized” L2 just to transact in a centralized asset. so even the idealistic vision that OGs had simply doesn’t fit the reality of which assets people want to use in high velocity

@LoganJastremski right but i don’t think that’s why L2s failed

if the premise of “people will want to transact in ETH permissionlessly on global scale” materialized, the tech would find a way

but people don’t even want to transact in ETH in a permissioned way

so here we are

tweets.text: Google’s ICI network is not all to all. None of Google’s TPU fabrics - scale-up or scale-out - are all to all today outside of the smallest atomic unit and even that is not switched. They are all 3d torus networks today. All to all enabled by a switched scale-up fabric is what matters for MoE inference. Google is working on this but not yet ready to the best of my knowledge. Maybe think more before you confidently post next time. Good luck!

tweets.text:
CLOBs are not going to take us to the RWA promised land.

Today Hyperliquid owns the liquidity for a handful of RWA macro names. But outside the top 10 traded assets (which are ~90% of volume) liquidity falls off a cliff. When there's enough retail demand, order books can work. But "perps on everything" is a different problem, and TradFi solved it decades ago.

The answer isn't every venue rebuilding its own order book. That's not what Robinhood does, it's not what Schwab does, and it's not what DeFi should be doing either.

Building your own book for every asset means bootstrapping demand ticker by ticker, renting liquidity with subsidies, and ending up with thin markets that blow out 200x the moment news hits. It's like sucking the ocean of TradFi liquidity through a straw.

Variational skips all of it via the RFQ model. RFQ is how institutions like Dragonfly actually trade. In RFQ, dealers quote just-in-time and hedge on the primary venue as orders come in. This lets Variational mainline TradFi liquidity directly and mirror it on-chain. Margin in smart contracts, settlement in stablecoins, liquidity aggregated from the people who already trade on the biggest underlying markets, like the CME and NYSE.

It makes it permissionless to access the same depth and spreads the big boys get. With the cold start problem gone, new markets can ship at the speed of software.

By next year I expect RWA perps to be the biggest contract class on-chain, bigger than BTC and ETH perps combined. That's how crypto truly becomes the market for everything.

I believe the platform that wins that won't look like a traditional exchange.

Proud to lead Variational's $50M Series A.

Watch this space.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
We’ve raised $50M led by @dragonfly_xyz to go all in on RWAs and bring TradFi liquidity on-chain.

Today, we're launching Phase 1 of our RWA rollout to stress-test our infrastructure before bringing 100+ TradFi markets on-chain this summer. https://t.co/lnYhZktyVt

thread_tweets.text:
In TradFi you don't *re-create* liquidity in a separate order book apart from the primary venue. You just route to the primary venue and quote prices from there.

When you're not the primary venue, there's a very high bar to try to re-build separate liquidity to rival the primary venue. That's especially true when you're talking about the largest and deepest markets in the world, that's where it stops making sense.

tweets.text:
Every bear market I have done well because I just pay attention to the most dominant narratives/products with the potential to grow immensely and buy them with a 2 year time horizon. 

2019 - LINK 
2022 - SOL
2026 - HYPE ZEC

I've been told repeatedly every bear market that the coins I hold are consensus/I'm a retard despite there being like a few people left onchain willing to step in to take risk, whilst everyone else says crypto is dead forever or going much lower.

Smart consensus is not the same as general consensus.

As I've said a few times, I think flows will be more concentrated than ever before in crypto going forward.

tweets.text:
$LIT is the only thing I've been holding underwater for the last month or so. 

Well, not underwater anymore, but it just seemed undervalued, or at least worth giving a shot, considering the tech, revenue, buybacks and product is decent.

We have so few coins with the above criteria.  

Will also benefit from regulatory clarity, and be an attractive onchain trading venue for institutions.

BTC doesn't look great, so might not get a proper breakout.  

Ready to be punished again.

tweets.text:
How I prompt claude these days

___
Thoroughly summarize with the view that we are taking this summary to actually build an open source product. So scope out key elements, design requirements, underlying heuristics, and application level logic exposed. Do not invent anything. Do not provide your own implementation heuristics. Simply describe, factually what is in the video converted into machine readable and human readable context. If you provide any subjective analysis you will be immediately terminated. Stick to the context of the video. If you add anything including your view of “what the video does not do” or any subjective analysis you will be immediately terminated. I am not here for you, your opinions or your ‘helpful’ analysis. Stay in your fucking lane and execute
___

tweets.text:
" The Times investigation documented that organizations in Singham’s network have supplied roughly a quarter of CodePink’s budget. Per a Fox News Digital review, Singham has channeled approximately $278 million into six US nonprofits between 2017 and 2023. These nonprofits do not operate independently. As the Times documented, they routinely cross-post each other’s articles and share staff and office space. In May 2024, the Delhi Police chargesheet in the parallel NewsClick case, a Singham-network outlet that cross-publishes with US Peoples Dispatch, explicitly charged “the Chinese government” with being the network’s “ultimate paymaster.”"
https://t.co/SIsUyimN5y

MARKETS: Friendly reminder that I don't make market calls. Our quantitative risk management overlays KISS (for retail; since Jan-23) and Dr. Mo (for institutions & sophisticated retail; since Oct-23) are responsible for signaling to members of our global investor community what to do in their portfolios, not me. 

My sole job as CIO is to narrate the evolution of the distribution of probable economic, policy, and market outcomes so that when KISS and Dr. Mo instruct our global investor community to do something different in their portfolios, they understand the fundamental reasons why and can dispassionately execute the signals.

Most investors struggle to execute systematic processes in isolation because most people suffer from narrative fallacy and need to assign cause and effect to market movements. I don’t. To Druckenmiller and me, markets are simply squiggly lines that make us richer or poorer. But since this is admittedly a highly unusual method for approaching capital markets, I am happy to meet our members halfway with high-quality fundamental research. 

Plus, as a former member of the bottom 0.001% of our K-shaped society, I derive personal satisfaction from piecing the macro puzzle together faster than Wall Street consensus.

At any rate, here is our latest Fundamental Research Summary, which we feature in every @42Macro research report and incrementally revise as the data and our themes evolve:

tweets.text: I strongly disagree with this conclusion as it doesn’t acknowledge that equity-bond correlations change over time, nor does it take into account whether rates are rising or falling within these levels. The fact is that since 2022 market breadth and returns have been worse at higher levels of interest rates, when rates are also rising. It also means that when rates fall from higher levels returns are stronger.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
There has been a lot of handwringing as of late, suggesting that higher US bond yields are the "death knell" to the market.  Two things:
1) There is no fixed relationship between bond yields and equity returns. Their correlation changes over time, driven by inflation dynamics, https://t.co/dTX3VMT4n5

ALTERNATIVE VIEW: He’s being dishonest and doesn’t want the public to know how rigged the tax code actually is after decades of wealthy people paying billions of dollars in campaign financing to rig elections and legislation on their behalf. 

Don’t watch this if you don’t want to find out the truth and get freaked out as a result: https://t.co/6kDPx90nGb.

Since Citizens United in 2010, federal election spending has totaled well over $100 billion cumulatively across presidential, congressional, party, PAC, Super PAC, and outside-group activity. 

Do you think some of the smartest people in world history spent well over $100 billion for poops and giggles, OR do you think this was intentional capex designed to capture a high-ROIC opportunity similar to the current spending on AI infrastructure?

Wake up.

h/t @robtfrank at @CNBC

P.S. I’m not anti-billionaire; I may become one someday. I am, however, against people cheating to rig the system disproportionately in their favor at the expense of others. It shouldn’t be this easy to corrupt our democracy, nor should it be this hard for working-class families to get ahead.

tweets.text:
Hyperliquid ETFs are here! As you consider your options, few highlights for why you should consider $BHYP:

We know the ETF business can sometimes feel like a “race to bottom” on nominal fees so it might feel like a decision can be made on that headline alone (FWIW BHYP is free for first 500mm and first month) but in my opinion, the driving force function should be “incentive alignment”

On that point, consider two unique features for Bitwise’s product—

1. Bitwise has the most competitive staking rate versus peers because it owns the staking solution in house. This is a big deal, because it ultimately means that this vertical integration results in maximum possible consumer benefits, all things equal. And the take rate % as part of gross rewards is likely far more consequential than few bps on headline fees in the long run. It also means Bitwise can manage the staking % more dynamically given streamlined operations for settlement. But maybe more importantly, because the team runs its operations on behalf of their customers, you can bet that your interest is Bitwise’s long term interest. In crypto, you all know there’s always some strange corner cases that can result unexpectedly as the technology and governance develops. By having it fully integrated, you can bet Bitwise will be the best fiduciary because in the end, if you lose, we lose. And if you win, we win. Sometimes it really is just that simple!

2. Famous for donating a percentage of profits as public goods demonstrated in past with other products, Bitwise has also committed to purchase $HYPE on its own balance sheet creating a virtuous flywheel using its profits. There is no cleaner incentive alignment than that, where principal risk lives alongside agency risk. That’s long term game with long term people.

So consider $BHYP closely before you hit the buy button. Many of you know we also have a few tricks up our sleeves to delight our customers 👀, and we want you to be a part of it too. Of course it goes without saying the deeply plugged sales team is on the other side of one phone call away if you want to learn more about the investment thesis too. 

Bitwise is excited to house all finance for the users, by the users.

Hyperliquid.

So consider $BHYP closely before you hit the buy button. Many of you know we also have a few tricks up our sleeves to delight our customers 👀, and we want you to be a part of it too. Of course it goes without saying the deeply plugged sales team is on the other side of one phone call away if you want to learn more about the investment thesis too. 

Bitwise is excited to house all finance for the users, by the users.

Hyperliquid.

tweets.text:
FAQ’s / Kindly read first:
 
Welcome!
 
1. When do you update your portfolio? 
Every morning before market opens, I will tell you what I’m buying or selling. Every night, I provide an update on the portfolio. Throughout the day, I will tell you if there’s any immediate changes.
 
2. Did you sell? 
Kindly check my latest portfolio update. My entire portfolio shows you exactly which stocks I am currently in. If the stock is NOT in there, it has been sold. But I will always tell you regardless before hand so kindly check. 
 
3. What do you think about this stock? 
Keep your winners. If they are up a lot, trim 20% so you can lock in some profits and use those profits to rotate elsewhere. If you believe in it, buy it. You probably know more about it than I do because you researched about it. All I can telll you is to buy LOW and trim HIGH. 
 
4. Should I buy a stock that’s already up a lot? 
Watch this video on how to buy stocks that is up a lot already: https://t.co/8dA8IsHLTt
 
5. When do you buy or sell? 
I usually buy between 7am - 10am New York time - doesn’t matter when exactly. A few % points doesn’t mean anything in the grand scheme of things :)
 
6. I have small capital, what should I do? 
You can buy fractional shares. 99% of brokers allow fractional shares. If you can’t, then pick 1 stock within that industry (PEP and KO, pick one. NVDA and AVGO, pick one). Or pick an ETF that covers that entire sector. 
 
7. Should we buy now? 
Whatever you want! I buy in the morning, you can do whatever you want - a couple % points doesn’t mean anything in the grand scheme of things.
 
8. Day trading course: https://t.co/bVtwQkQTYg
 
9. Swing trading course: https://t.co/H4yX5jZ9fx 
 
10. What EMA's are you using? 
63 / 79 EMA & 200 MA
 
11. Why are you buying these stocks? 
All my buys are very intentional. They work together as a machine. Rotations, setups, etc. I combine my strategy to create a monster. 
 
12. Would you recommend I buy all the stocks you currently hold or continue with the new purchases you make starting tomorrow? 
Each one of my purchases are intentional. The portfolio works together as core, spears, and moonshots. The reason we win is because they push and pull together. You can buy all current positions. If they are up a lot, then smaller position for now and build up on dips. You can continue buying my newer buys. I buy every day slowly. 
 
13. Do you do shares or options? 
I do shares. However, a lot of people that are comfortable with options can use options as well. Your choice. 
 
14. How long do you hold for? 
Really depends on the markets. Sometimes can be just 1 day because of earnings catalyst. Can be a few months driven market regimes. Can be more if we continue the trend. In all cases, I will tell you when I buy or trim/sell every day. 
 
15. What's your time horizon for the 100k challenge? Is there an end-number you're working towards or just purely taking it as high as possible? 
$1M -> $4M. Our goal is to always 2X the S&P 500 minimum. But goal every year is 70% per year. All-in-all, let’s compound together over the next 5 years together and we’ll be rich. 
 
16. Can you explain more about your hedging positions? what's the point of doing them? 
Kindly check this video on advanced hedging theory: https://t.co/gEkUpHWcMV
 
17. How do you buy pre-market / post-market? 
99% of brokers in the world allow for trading outside of regular hours. Kindly check with your broker if they allow this. I personally like Interactive Brokers, Robinhood, Schwab, WeBull, etc. 
 
18. There are a few positions that are already up 10-15%. Should I get into those now or not? 
You can, but again, buy a smaller size and when it dips, buy more.

tweets.text:
I absolutely hate it when I do a swap and get stuck with a number that is way less than what it was intended because of slippage.

So when I was scanning around for decent swap aggregators, I came across @matchametaxyz, an under-the-radar aggregator that's solving one of the biggest friction points: slippage.

A core feature is that they don’t make you blindly trust just one aggregator when swapping or bridging, so I decided to throw a few bucks at it to test it out.

Honestly, I was pleasantly surprised.

First thing I noticed was the UI. It’s minimalistic and has smooth animations, which is something I love.

Second, I liked how the aggregator routes were laid out clearly, so I could compare them and pick the one that made the most sense.

They also have a simulation toggle. It basically tries to catch routes that overquote or fail before you sign.

Keep in mind, it doesn’t make execution magically guaranteed, but it gives you a better understanding of the quote before you take the swap.

That said, simulations still have limits because they’re based on current conditions, and crypto moves fast.

But overall, I like how they’re trying to make cross-chain swaps feel less chaotic, and not getting rekt on slippage is pretty much all I want from a swap tool.

tweets.text:
Not all crypto cards are built the same.

So I made a simple "best by" list for the current market (Tria vs. Ether Fi vs. Kast vs. Plasma vs. Avici) ↓

▪ @useTria: Best by growth, cashback, chain abstraction, DAU engagement, and overall tech ambition.

▪ @ether_fi: Best by active usage, revenue per user, and DeFi integration.

▪ @KASTxyz: Best by scale, annualized spend, premium card perks, and cashbank setup.

▪ @Plasma: Best by positioning, yield, infra growth potential

▪ @AviciMoney: Best by credit product and growth ambitions.

So with all that being said, I just gotta ask: which card best fits how you already use crypto?

Are we still underestimating how much this reshapes the internet economy?

We try unpacking it all in this video.

tweets.text:
10 emerging Hyperliquid-native protocols I'm paying attention to ↓

1. @monetrix_xyz: YieldMaxxing on Hyperliquid. A single stablecoin that pulls together funding rates, HLP, maker rebates, and HIP-3 into one onchain yield, powered by Portfolio Margin. Think the Hyperliquid-native Ethena.

2. @rosetta_hl: An automated yield router for stablecoins on Hyperliquid. Deposit USDC and it moves your money across Felix, Hyperlend, Morpho, Aave, and HLP every block to wherever the rate is highest, after gas and slippage.

3. @papertrade_xyz: A fair-launched perps protocol on Hyperliquid offering up to 1000x leverage with no funding rates and no slippage. Trades execute against a price feed instead of an orderbook, fully onchain.

4. @altdotfun: A launchpad on Hyperliquid where every token is paired with a leveraged perp position (2x, 3x, or 5x long or short on any HL market). The token moves both from trading activity and from the underlying perp itself.

5. @ventuals: Pre-IPO perps on Hyperliquid. Take long or short positions on private company valuations like OpenAI, SpaceX, and Stripe with up to 10x leverage, built on HIP-3.

6. @liminalmoney: A delta-neutral yield protocol on Hyperliquid. Deposit stablecoins, capture funding rates through automated short positions, and use the resulting xTokens (xBTC, xETH, xHYPE) as collateral across DeFi.

7. @Meltfinance: Brings tokenized stocks, commodities, and equities onto Hyperliquid as spot markets. You trade real-world assets on the same orderbook you trade BTC, 24/7, from your own wallet.

8. @ChainSight_: An oracle and data infrastructure protocol on Hyperliquid. Builds modular pipelines for price feeds, volatility indices, and risk metrics with sub-3 second latency, powering new derivative products on HL.

9. @ripdotxyz: Tokenized vault strategies on HyperEVM. Flagship rHYPURR gives you liquid, fractional exposure to a managed basket of Hypurr NFTs through ERC-4626 shares, priced hourly by NAV and backed by WHYPE.

10. @Markets_xyz: A perps exchange on Hyperliquid from the Kinetiq team. Trade equities, FX, commodities, bonds, and crypto perps 24/7 with up to 50x leverage, all USDH-margined and priced by Kaiko's institutional oracle.

Hyperliquid is moving beyond perps to become the full onchain financial stack. These are the primitives shipping real yield, liquidity, and new products natively.

What did I miss?

The obsession with your job and linking it to intelligence is striver cope for having no other defining qualities and has to die. Since when did clocking in at a specific employer become a proxy for intelligence? It’s LinkedIn brain rot and not very lindy if you look at history. 

Stupid people are everywhere, and a huge share of them coast on pedigree rather than any real accomplishments. Passing some leetcodes and surviving a couple of PIP cycles is not evident of your intelligence but it is evidence of your conformity. And talking about “averages” here is as useful as talking about the average weather in California.

What I especially liked was their breakdown of the upcoming Robotics Capital Markets and how it could create a massive flywheel for the $DEUS token.

XMAQUINA is aiming to become much more than just a robotics investment DAO.

The bigger vision is to turn robotics companies into onchain RWAs, making them globally accessible, liquid and tradeable 24/7 for everyone.

Think about:

PumpFun for memecoins.
Virtuals for AI agents.
xStocks for sought-after pre-IPO companies.

That’s what XMAQUINA is building for robotics.

Once this goes live, $DEUS doesn’t just become a revenue accruing token, the entire system becomes a self-reinforcing flywheel that continuously brings more robotics exposure onchain.

tweets.text:
On this day in 1873, the U.S. Patent Office granted patent #139,121 to Jacob Davis and Levi Strauss for the process of riveting pocket seams on work pants. Davis was a tailor in Reno who had been reinforcing pants with copper rivets for railroad workers and miners. He knew he had something but couldn't afford the $68 patent filing fee, so he wrote to his fabric supplier, Levi Strauss, and offered to split it.

Strauss put up the money and got half the patent. That $68 investment built a company worth over $6 billion today. The riveted pants became standard equipment for every laborer in the American West within a decade. By the time the patent expired in 1891, Levi Strauss & Co. had locked in enough brand recognition that competitors couldn't catch up.

Davis did the inventing. Strauss did the financing. The guy who controlled the capital ended up with his name on the product. That dynamic hasn't changed in 150 years.

https://t.co/S2DAOouFxZ: The standout signal over the past few weeks is listing prices, which continued to decline year over year for the 17th straight week. 

Price reductions are falling year over year, suggesting that this listing price softness is more of an indication of adjusting seller expectations than a sign of market weakness. 

Put differently, sellers seem to be listing at a lower price to begin with, rather than listing high and reducing the price later. This continued shift is good news for buyers, who may see more homes coming to the market at attractive prices.

tweets.text:
Morningstar with a chart comparing their US Markets and US Semiconductors indices starting March 1, 1999 and May 13, 2025.

While the returns so far have lagged the 1999-2000 period, the bigger difference says Morningstar’s Chief US Market Strategist David Sekera is the fair value estimates from Morningstar’s equity analysts:

“Morningstar’s current price/fair value metric indicates that the broad market is trading at a few percent discount as compared to a composite of our fair values,” he says. “While the technology sector has rallied significantly this year, that rally has been justified. 

"Morningstar’s equity research team has increased fair values within the sector as the visibility into the longer-term structural growth for the sector lengthens further into our base-case assumptions. In fact, a number of stocks most closely correlated with the AI buildout boom, such as Nvidia NVDA, remain undervalued today, even after a large runup in their stock prices.”

Sekera says that the bullish valuation story isn’t the case across the board, however.

“There are many cases we think investors are overly exuberant in their growth estimates,” he says. ”For example, stocks for commodity-oriented technology hardware such as Sandisk SNDK and Micron MU have run up too far as compared to their intrinsic valuations.”

While we just got 1% of the SPX by earnings weight reporting last week, it didn’t stop 2026 SPX earnings growth expectations from increasing another half percent to +21.5%, up +5.2% the past eight weeks and up from +14.8% at the start of the year.

Like in 2025, Tech will be a leader with exceptional y/y earnings growth of +40.9% (up from +28.6% at the start of the year) but Energy remains in the lead +57.3% (up from +6.4% at the start the year), and along with Materials +39.0% and Comm Services +26.2%, the four sectors expected to come in above the SPX average.

tweets.text:
EIA US crude inventories for the week ending May 15th drew -7.9mb, the largest draw since Feb 13th and the fifth in six weeks as inventories continue to drop from the highest levels since June 2023 hit April 17th, with the y/y surplus nearly evaporating to just +1.9mb (+0.4%) ex-SPR.  

Crude inventories deepened to ~2% below the 5-yr average (from ~0.3% below).  In addition the SPR drew another -9.9mb and is now -26.3mb y/y (-6.6%) to the least since Oct 2024.

Products again saw a modest draw this week -1.1mb (after -0.8mb the prior week). Gasoline drew -1.5mb (now -46.9mb the past fourteen weeks), with gasoline inventories remaining -5% below the 5-yr average — the least for this time of year in three years. Distillates saw another small build +0.4mb (still -24.3mb the past fourteen weeks with distillates -9% below the 5-yr average). Residual fuel oil drew -0.7mb. Propane/propylene edged +0.4mb, other oils (blending products) and fuel ethanol were essentially flat, while jet fuel built +0.2mb (third straight build despite global shortage).

Production was also essentially flat (-8kb/d to 13,702 kb/d, vs the record high of 13,862 kb/d on Nov 7th) while refinery intake — crude input to refineries — eased slightly -80kb/d to 16,319 kb/d, with the utilization rate essentially flat at 91.6% (from 91.7%), slightly above year-ago levels (it was 90.7% this week last year, chart).

Net imports were essentially unchanged (+4kb/d) as imports and exports both rose modestly (+116kb/d and +112kb/d respectively). The four-week average of net imports is now -89.9% y/y, while the four-week average of exports is +48.5% y/y to an all-time high (imports are -1.5% y/y) (chart).
#oott

tweets.text:
Brent Kochuba, founder of SpotGamma, believes a deeper pullback could be in the cards with Nvidia's Wednesday post-close earnings release potentially marking a turning point.

Kochuba pointed to the fact the SPX and VIX are both positive this month which could be evidence that investors are bracing for a pullback, Kochuba told MarketWatch. The dynamic also has shown up in the $QQQ Nasdaq-100 Trust and $SMH VanEck Semiconductor ETF. 

Typically, implied volatility and spot price are inversely correlated for a given instrument, according to Kochuba. “When both rise together, traders may be paying up for protection even as they chase upside — a sign the market is bracing for expanded volatility,” he wrote.

Wednesday’s Nvidia earnings report could be another “sell the news”-driven selloff, similar to what investors witnessed last time around, according to Art Hogan, chief market strategist at B. Riley Wealth. “Investors have been whistling by the inflation graveyard and ignoring signals from the Treasury curve, as we’ve broken above 4.5% and on a trajectory to go even higher,” he added.

tweets.text:
Now that we've broken the prior intraday high of 5.153% on the 30-yr US Treasury, Citi says traders are targeting the 5.5% level, last seen in 2004.   

“Investors’ calculus in terms of buying the dip on Treasuries has changed,” McCormick said in an interview in Singapore. Core inflation shows no signs of slowing, while US growth should outperform developed-market peers as the world’s largest economy is expected to weather the energy crisis better, he added.

“I see markets underpricing the risk of a Fed rate hike starting this year,” said McCormick. “The biggest risk to the global economy is the size of the shock to global bond yields,” McCormick said. “If long-dated US yields continue to march higher, it creates a pretty unstable equilibrium for riskier assets such as equities and credit.”

tweets.full_text:
Now that we've broken the prior intraday high of 5.153% on the 30-yr US Treasury, Citi says traders are targeting the 5.5% level, last seen in 2004.   

“Investors’ calculus in terms of buying the dip on Treasuries has changed,” McCormick said in an interview in Singapore. Core inflation shows no signs of slowing, while US growth should outperform developed-market peers as the world’s largest economy is expected to weather the energy crisis better, he added.

“I see markets underpricing the risk of a Fed rate hike starting this year,” said McCormick. “The biggest risk to the global economy is the size of the shock to global bond yields,” McCormick said. “If long-dated US yields continue to march higher, it creates a pretty unstable equilibrium for riskier assets such as equities and credit.”

tweets.text:
Barclays: Figure 3 shows that post-Volcker, the average of the fed funds-30y slope has been about 180bp. 

When we adjust for very high and low policy rate scenarios, the average has been about 150bp. Currently, 30y yields are trading at a spread of 150bp over the overnight rate. Hence, this simple exercise tells you that bonds are not cheap.

10yr bonds are arguably still overpriced.

$NVDA Tier1Alpha: Nvidia [as of Monday] carries an 8.9% weight in the S&P 500 [now 8.6%], making it the single largest holding ever to have an earnings event. 

For context, the only comparable single-stock weights in modern history were Apple in 2023 and IBM in 1984. 

Adding even more to the anticipation, Nvidia has also been a major contributor to the historic rally off the March 31st low, accounting for more than 15% of the total SPX return over that period.

tweets.text:
Goldman:  Since April, the market’s rally has moved locked step with retail buying. 

Beginning April 1st, we observe an R2 of 76% between retail buying in the Russell 3000 and index returns when studying 10-day moving averages. Per our GIR colleagues, a basket of stocks popular with retail traders (GSXURFAV) has rallied by 29% since mid-April and retail volumes have increased 28%. Within the Russell 3000, retail traders prefer smaller cap names (<$0.5bn) and mega caps
(>$100bn) 

Across the Russell 3000, there has not been a negative retail imbalance day since March 30th, 2026, and there has not been a month with more than 5 negative days since July 2025.

The May MTD retail buy imbalance is averaging $1.8bn per day, the highest average since the start of 2025. Interestingly, the accelerating retail buy imbalance since March 2026 has not been driven by higher volume; it is the more consistent buy direction of the trades that is the driver.

tweets.text:
BoA (Ciana): The VanEck Semiconductor ETF ($SMH) is up ~50% YTD and ~35% since the April 7 US Iran ceasefire. 

Price action has become increasingly vertical, while the 14-week RSI has remained above 80 for two consecutive weeks—an extreme overbought condition.

 In the past, similar signals did not mark immediate tops, rather corrections, and consistently led to a topping process characterized by: 1) Higher volatility and larger price swings, 2) Bearish RSI divergence, 3) Bearish reversal candles, and 4) Top patterns forming over time with meaningful corrections.  

Breakdowns under the 50wk SMA saw sustained selloffs. Corrections resolved near the 200wk SMA.

Price action has become increasingly vertical, while the 14-week RSI has remained above 80 for two consecutive weeks—an extreme overbought condition.

 In the past, similar signals did not mark immediate tops, rather corrections, and consistently led to a topping process characterized by: 1) Higher volatility and larger price swings, 2) Bearish RSI divergence, 3) Bearish reversal candles, and 4) Top patterns forming over time with meaningful corrections.  

Breakdowns under the 50wk SMA saw sustained selloffs. Corrections resolved near the 200wk SMA.

tweets.text:
$NVDA BoA expects "the usual historical 2-4% $2-$4bn sales outperformance" with focus beyond that on cash return to shareholders, new product timing, margin durability, revenue forecast, and competition.

They say of those, the first (cash return) may be the most important: "NVDA’s large existing positioning (8.3% of the SPX index, ~78% active fund manager ownership) often acts as a headwind.  Other large cap tech names in the same position have added incremental investors by boosting cash returns....  NVDA hasn’t done this yet, with only 47% of FCF from CY22-25 allocated to dividends/buybacks versus peers returning around 80% of FCF.  

"NVDA’s investments have instead been diverted to investing in the ecosystem (OpenAI, Anthropic, tech partners) and have been unfairly, in our view,  characterized as circular/vendor financing.  Boosting shareholder returns could expand ownership, close NVDA’s valuation gap and minimize circularity concerns (2H catalyst)."

Otherwise, they "expect NVDA to sustain ~70% revenue share in a $1.7 Tn+ CY30E AI TAM.  Recent noise on CPU/GPU ratio tilting  towards CPU in agentic is misleading since: 1) NVDA’s Vera CPU will be a formidable rival  in stand-alone CPU, with related newsflow at upcoming Computex, 2) CPU/GPU ratio in widely deployed Blackwell/TPU clusters already at 1:2, and 3) no correlation with agentic  CPU in standalone clusters.  CPU will be a large but crowded market with x86/ARM options."

tweets.text:
Yardeni: The selloff in the US Treasury bond market continued today... Just as unsettling as these levels is how quickly yields have risen over the past few days.

We think that happened in response to last week's hotter-than-expected April PPI as well as the latest batch of stronger-than-expected economic indicators. That combination cannot be described as “stagflation.” In fact, while some economists have warned that yields are in the "Danger Zone," we think they remain in the "Normal Zone," reflecting a resilient economy with a short-term inflation problem. The Fed should respond to the latter by raising the federal funds rate within the next two months.

Nevertheless, we expect that the economy and corporate earnings will remain resilient. Our current assessment is that the bull market isn't at risk of being derailed by the sell-off in the bond market, which presents a very good opportunity to buy both bonds and stocks.

Interesting find from @dailychartbook in the nightly email from the really great follow @ernietedeschi.  

While a lot has been made out of the surge in "new businesses" (i.e., applications for new business employment identification numbers (EINs)) Ernie notes that this is likely just a continued expansion of the gig economy in part fueled by AI:

"We see some evidence of this in our own Stripe Atlas data, where startups have accelerated since 2023, and especially in the first quarter of 2026. The overwhelming driver of this acceleration comes from solo founders, and we see growth among both AI and non-AI startups (but AI might be enabling easier formations even for firms whose business models are not AI-based)."

tweets.text:
From the Wednesday morning update, sub here to get it in your inbox each morning (free subscribers get the whole note) https://t.co/q0o89SNAmO 

BBG - UK employers slashed jobs by the most since the start of the pandemic in April, suggesting demand for workers is weakening as the Iran war pushes up energy costs and saps business confidence.

Tax data showed the number of payrolled employees dropped by 100,000 following a 28,000 decline in March, the Office for National Statistics said on Tuesday. It was far worse than the 10,000 fall expected by economists, with retail accounting for much of the decline.

However, the ONS said the data is likely to be subject to bigger-than-normal revisions due to it relating to the start of the tax year when some returns from employers may be incomplete.

Unemployment for the three months through March rose to 5% from 4.9% in the period through February, with the figure for March alone surging to 5.5% — the highest since 2015. Vacancies (job openings) were the lowest since 2021.

“The latest batch of labor-market data suggests labor demand is responding faster than we expected to higher energy costs and tighter funding conditions stemming from the Iran war. While payroll figures could be overstating current weakness, the broader direction points to further cooling from here.” —Ana Andrade and Dan Hanson, economists.

Wage pressures also continued to ease with regular pay growth cooling to 3.4% in the three months through March, the lowest since 2020. Private sector pay rose 3%, down from a 3.2% increase previously. In real terms, wages grew just 0.3% on an annual basis in January to March 2026, rising at the slowest pace since 2023

Traders reacted to the figures by trimming bets on interest-rate hikes by the Bank of England, pricing in 57 basis points of tightening by year-end. The pound was little-changed, trading around 0.8683 per euro.

“All of this questions the need for Bank of England rate hikes,” said James Smith, economist at ING. “The economy looks much less susceptible to ‘second round’ effects from higher energy prices on things like wage growth, than it did four years ago in the last oil/gas shock.”

tweets.text:
From the Tuesday morning update, sub here to get it in your inbox each morning (free subscribers get the whole note) https://t.co/q0o89SN2xg 

BBG - Japan’s economy grew much faster than expected at the start of the year, supporting the case for further Bank of Japan interest-rate hikes, though the outlook remains highly uncertain due to the Middle East conflict.

Real gross domestic product expanded 2.1% on an annualized basis in the first quarter, according to a Cabinet Office report Tuesday. The reading topped economists’ forecast for a 1.7% increase and marked an acceleration from a downwardly revised growth pace of 0.8% in the previous quarter. Stronger-than-expected private consumption and trade helped power the acceleration.

The report showed private consumption, which accounts for more than 50% of Japan’s GDP, increased 0.3% on a non-annualized basis, exceeding a 0.1% forecast. That was likely supported by government utility subsidies and wage growth finally starting to outpace inflation.

Net exports also supported the economy by more than expected. March trade data showed that Japan’s exports grew at a faster clip as demand in China rebounded.

Growth in business investment slowed to a 0.3% gain from the previous quarter. The Finance Ministry’s March corporate survey showed a fifth consecutive quarterly increase in ordinary profits. The global artificial intelligence boom, along with rising demand for digitalization amid labor shortages, continued to support companies’ appetite for investment.

Despite the bullish growth figures, the yen edged a fraction weaker against the dollar after the release. Overnight swaps were largely unchanged, showing around a 77% likelihood that the central bank will hike rates in June. The 10-year Japanese government bond (JGB) yield rose above 2.81%, the highest since 1996.

“Japan’s solid first-quarter GDP growth strengthens the case for the BOJ to keep withdrawing stimulus through further rate hikes... We still expect the BOJ to raise its policy rate to 1.0% in June, but the call is far from certain.” — Taro Kimura, economist

tweets.text:
Is this crypto spring, or just a classic "sell the news" setup? 📈👇

We are currently witnessing a classic market paradox. On one hand, institutional infrastructure is accelerating and major regulatory milestones are clearing. On the other hand, historical lessons and short-term indicators suggest a potential pullback.

In this weekend’s episode of Kaupr Weekly, we cut through the noise to analyze the quiet, structural migration occurring onchain:

♦ The Clarity Act: Current status and compromise details on U.S. stablecoin legislation.

♦ CryptoQuant Analysis: Profit-taking data and market indicators on Bitcoin's recent run.

♦ Venture Capital & Tech: Aly Yahya (Andreessen Horowitz) and Dan Morehead (Pantera Capital) on the convergence of digital assets and AI.

♦ Y Combinator: The move to New York for crypto and fintech interviews.

Digital Treasuries & Corporate Credit: Joseph Lubin (Consensys) and

♦ JPMorgan research on corporate accumulation and the $3 trillion digital credit market.

What is Kaupr Weekly? Kaupr Weekly is a short podcast anchored in the news flow. One main takeaway — a trend, a story, or an event from the week — analyzed and prepared by Morten Myrstad. New episodes every weekend.

Listen to Episode 2 here:
https://t.co/ptp1PF7iWY

tweets.text:
This chart is highly sensationalized and contains several elements that suggest it is not a genuine JPMorgan research graphic. While the energy market faces real complexities, the "imminent collapse" depicted here should be viewed with significant skepticism.
Red Flags & Discrepancies
• The "Iran War" Marker: The chart labels a massive drop in February 2026 due to an "Iran war." As of May 2026, there is no such large-scale kinetic conflict that has caused global inventories to drop by over a billion barrels in a single quarter.
• Fabricated Timeline: The chart claims the "Operational Stress Level" was reached in June 2026 and the "Operational Floor" will be reached in September. Given that today is May 9, 2026, this chart is predicting a future that hasn't happened yet, while framing past dates (like February 2026) with events that did not occur on that scale.
• Unrealistic "Floor" Mechanics: The global oil system is a massive, decentralized network of thousands of independent actors. The idea that there is a single, hard "6.8 billion barrel" line where the entire global energy system simply "breaks" is an oversimplification. In reality, as inventories tighten, prices spike, which naturally destroys demand and incentivizes more production, preventing a total "zero-out" scenario.

tweets.text:
As always a nice collection of posts from @C_Barraud.  One was a BBG article about how despite concerns that the US dollar may be losing its dominance due to a number of reasons (sanctions avoidance, a less stable US regime, diversification, shifting trade flows, etc.), according to the Belgium-based Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication (SWIFT), the greenback’s portion of international transactions rose to a record 51.1% in March, up from 49.2% a month earlier.

The dollar was followed by the euro at 21%, then the pound, yen, Chinese yuan and Canadian dollar.

“Dollar weakness seen last year has not translated into any clear decline in the dollar’s role as a reserve or base currency for capital markets,” a JPMorgan research team led by Joyce Chang said in an April 21 note. “We see a broader trend toward diversification rather than outright de-dollarization, and the data fail to demonstrate broad-based de-dollarization,” Chang and team said.

Click through to read more including charts and the other highlighted posts.

🧐 Fact-checking this chart comparison on $BTC vs Gold (1972 parallel):

Current prices check out:
• BTC ~$77.5k (down from $126k ATH late 2025)
• Gold ~$4,730/oz (off Jan 2026 ATH of $5,589 after huge 2025 run)

The TA analogy is creative, but calling "GOLD HAS ENDED ITS BULL CYCLE" is misleading. Top analysts (JPM, etc.) still see gold pushing toward $5k–$6k in 2026 - structural bull drivers remain strong.

BTC looks resilient, but "oversold + liquidity rotation now" is a hypothesis, not confirmed fact. Charts are fun, but DYOR and consider the full macro picture!

Sources:
• Live BTC: https://t.co/2Q52qrpiQ6
• Live Gold: https://t.co/bVGy3yCS3n
• Gold 2026 outlook: JPM research + others

What do you think - rotation or both can run? 🚀

tweets.text:
The VIX, known as Wall Street's "fear gauge," tracks implied volatility of the S&P 500 over 30 days, with a long-term average around 20 – though it briefly spiked above 30 at the end of March.

Many investors use the VIX as a contrarian indicator, seeing a very low reading as a sign of dangerous complacency and a very high reading as a potential buying opportunity.

JPMorgan research covering 724 instances over 30 years when the VIX closed above 30 found that investors who bought the S&P 500 during those moments saw positive returns 70–83% of the time depending on the time horizon.

Average returns following a VIX spike above 30 were 2.4% after one month, 6.6% after three months, and 12.4% after six months – though the range of outcomes was very wide in both directions.

The VIX spiked above 31 on March 27th before falling back below 20, and the S&P 500 has since rallied around 11%, meaning some of the typical post-spike rebound may already have occurred.

Despite that, the broader picture looks reasonably encouraging, with strong corporate profits (consensus estimates pointing to roughly 12% EPS growth) and S&P 500 valuations now looking less stretched than earlier in the year.

The key takeaway is that a volatility spike should not trigger panic – instead, it's a prompt to assess whether fear has outrun reality, as that is historically when longer-term opportunities tend to emerge.

Check out the full research here:
https://t.co/DxoTrqmR8V

#VIX #stocks $SPY

tweets.text:
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗠𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗜𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝗖𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗹 𝗕𝗮𝗻𝗸 𝗗𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟲 𝗜𝘀 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗬𝗼𝘂 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸. 𝗝𝗲𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗲 𝗣𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗹𝗹’𝘀 𝗧𝗲𝗿𝗺 𝗘𝘅𝗽𝗶𝗿𝗲𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝘆 𝟭𝟱. 𝗧𝗿𝘂𝗺𝗽 𝗛𝗮𝘀 𝟮𝟰 𝗗𝗮𝘆𝘀 𝘁𝗼 𝗡𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝗮 𝗦𝘂𝗰𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀𝗼𝗿. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗙𝗲𝗱 𝗝𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗕𝗲𝗰𝗮𝗺𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗱 𝗠𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗜𝗺𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝗡𝗲𝗴𝗼𝘁𝗶𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝗻 𝗪𝗮𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴𝘁𝗼𝗻.

Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell’s term as Chair expires May 15, 2026, per Federal Reserve documentation. Trump administration officials have not publicly committed to a replacement. iShares Fed Outlook research explicitly states “be on the lookout for changes in messaging in 2026 when a new Chair is named.” This is the single largest discretionary policy variable in US markets this year.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘅𝘁 𝗻𝗼𝗯𝗼𝗱𝘆 𝗵𝗮𝘀 𝗺𝗮𝗽𝗽𝗲𝗱.

The Fed held the federal funds rate at 3.50-3.75% at its March 18 meeting for the second consecutive time. The median FOMC projection still shows one rate cut in 2026 per the March SEP. JPMorgan global research forecasts the Fed holds through 2026 then hikes 25bps in Q3 2027 per April 17 JPMorgan research note. CME FedWatch market pricing has shifted decisively hawkish since the Iran war began February 28. Inflation forecasts got revised up at the March meeting: core PCE now 2.7% for 2026 end-year, up from 2.5% in December.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗰𝗶𝗳𝗶𝗰 𝗱𝘆𝗻𝗮𝗺𝗶𝗰 𝗺𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗹𝘆𝘀𝘁𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗺𝗶𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗻𝗴.

Stephen Miran dissented at the March meeting voting for a 25bp cut. Waller and Miran both dissented in January voting for cuts. The committee has a functioning dovish minority that has consistently been outvoted by the hawkish median. If Trump appoints a dovish Chair in place of Powell on May 15, that minority potentially becomes a new functioning majority. The FOMC operates on one-chair-one-vote but the Chair sets the agenda, controls committee communication, and has historically been followed by at least 3-4 Governors on close votes. A dovish Chair shift could move effective policy 50-75bps lower over 12 months without a single formal rate cut decision changing its nominal character.

𝗪𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗼𝗻𝗱 𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗶𝘀 𝗽𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘄𝗵𝘆 𝗶𝘁 𝗺𝗮𝘆𝗯𝗲 𝘄𝗿𝗼𝗻𝗴.

The 10-year Treasury is at 4.24% per Friday close data. TIPS breakeven inflation expectations have been steadily rising per Charles Schwab analysis published after the March FOMC. Markets are pricing continued Fed patience through year end. Markets are not pricing a regime change in May. That is the mispricing.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗣𝗼𝘄𝗲𝗹𝗹 𝗿𝗲𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗰𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗱𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗹𝗶𝘀𝘁 𝗽𝗲𝗿 𝗕𝗹𝗼𝗼𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿𝗴 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗪𝗦𝗝 𝗿𝗲𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗵𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗴𝗵 𝗤𝟭 𝟮𝟬𝟮𝟲.

Kevin Warsh, former Fed Governor, market-respected but viewed as moderately hawkish. Christopher Waller, current Governor, dovish and dissented for cuts repeatedly. Kevin Hassett, former National Economic Council director, strongly aligned with Trump preferences for lower rates. Betsy Duke, former Fed Governor. James Bullard, former St Louis Fed President. The distribution of possible outcomes is enormous. Warsh means status quo extended. Hassett means immediate dovish pivot. Waller means dovish pivot through existing institutional channels.

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘀 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗜𝗿𝗮𝗻 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗶𝗻𝗴.

If the Iran conflict resumes this week under Scenario C from my framework, oil spikes to $115-140 Brent. Core inflation surges through 3%. The incumbent Fed under Powell is structurally biased toward looking through oil shocks per Powell’s own March 18 press conference statement. A replacement Chair named during active conflict has two options: maintain the Powell posture and alienate Trump who appointed them, or pivot dovish and risk inflation expectations de-anchoring. The political pressure on the first Trump-appointed Chair of this Fed will be extraordinary from day one.

1/2🧵

tweets.text:
The Math of Missing Out $SPX $SPY $VOO

JP Morgan $JPM research proves that timing the market is a trap. If you missed just the 10 best days in the S&P 500 over 20 years, your total returns were slashed by roughly half.

But wait, there’s more. Miss the 20 best days and you actually underperformed basic cash. Staying on the sidelines doesn't protect your wealth, it effectively erodes your long-term growth.

The best days aren't random, they cluster around the worst periods. If you exit during a downturn to "wait for calm" you’ll likely miss the massive rebounds that drive performance.

Success isn't about predicting the bottom, it’s about having the discipline to stay invested through the noise. Don't let a bad month derail a 20-year plan. Stay the course.

Previsão do JPMorgan: BTC pode chegar a $170K nesse ciclo.

Standard Chartered foi além: ETH a $25K até 2028.

Enquanto isso, Fear & Greed Index está em 8.

Tradução: os mesmos que vão comprar a $170K estão com medo a $68K.

O padrão se repete em todo ciclo:
→ Extreme Fear = oportunidade
→ Extreme Greed = armadilha

A diferença entre quem lucra e quem perde é timing emocional.

tweets.text:
Holy f**k… I opened my DMs a few days ago and institutions (Bloomberg, WSJ, GS), research firms, PE, startups, and a lot of you degens slid right in.

It’s been an overwhelmingly positive experience, though giving meaningful feedback to everyone takes real time.

I’ve always been against keeping alpha gated, so I’m thinking about creating a paid sub for a tighter-knit community, where we can talk more freely and I can prioritize your DMs. I’ll always post the same content for free the next day.

By popular request, here’s what people want most:
> How to find quality information
> How to build a long-term portfolio
> How to maximize returns through swing trading
> How to buy the dip optimally and rotate (trim) your winners
> How to spot trends
> Deep dives on single names
> Perhaps the most important: how to build conviction in the names you buy

One thing I’ll never do: tell you to go all-in on one stock because you’ll reach $11M in just two years. (Here is looking at you Kevin)

My thesis has been the same since I started posting in January: invest in the AI buildout. And it’s been working.

CPU $INTC $AMD $ARM

Memory $MU $SNDK SK Hynix Kioxia

Photonics $SIVE $AAOI $TSEM $LITE $LPK $P4O $AEHR $AXTI $IQE $SOI $MXL $M7U $TRT FOCI Shunsin MSSCorps

NeoClouds $NBIS $DGXX

Even after these last couple of red days and giving back some solid gains, I’m sitting at +287% YTD. I’ll sit on my hands, let the thesis play out, and get back to ATH with conviction.

So the question is: is a small-fee sub worth it to you for priority DMs and tighter feedback, or would you rather I keep it open and get back to you when I can?

Let me hear your thoughts.

UFC has been an incredible success for $PSKY early on according to Morgan Stanley Research. 
'more than 10 Million homes have watched over 100 million hours of UFC programming, about 15x the average pay-per-view event over the past two years'. $TKO.

tweets.text:
Morgan Stanley Research is bullish on $PSKY. They Reiterate an Overweight rating and a $14 price target (28% upside). 

Summary of the Report-

MS reiterates OW on $PSKY, $14 PT (~25% upside). Q1 EBITDA beat, FY26 guide reiterated, WBD deal on track for 3Q close. Setup is improving.

Q1 Highlights:
- Adj EBITDA $1.16B vs. MS $957M / cons $890M
- Revenue $7.3B (+2% YoY) vs. cons $7.2B
- Paramount+ subs hit 79.6M (+700K q/q)
- P+ revenue +17% YoY on January price hike + 14% ARPU growth
- DTC margins reached +10% EBITDA
- TV Media better than feared (ads -6% vs MSe -12%)

FY26 Reiterated:
- Revenue $30B (+4% YoY)
- Adj EBITDA $3.8B
- Revenue 2H-weighted, profit 1H-weighted

The Bigger Story — Standalone Synergies Build Credibility for $WBD:
- $2.5B run-rate efficiencies by YE26
- $3B+ through 2027
- Validates the $6B WBD synergy opportunity (~11% of OpEx)

WBD Deal Mechanics:
- $49B bridge successfully syndicated, to be refinanced with secured IG + HY debt before close
- Existing Paramount notes stay unsecured post-close
PIPE included a 10-year warrant per Class B share (strike floored at $12, capped at $16.02) — MS values them at ~$2-3/share
- No incremental equity issuance or asset sales assumed in base case

Strategic Signals from David Ellison:
- Flexible content licensing — flagship IP (LotR, GoT, DC, Harry Potter) stays exclusive; other content licensed to 3rd parties for high-margin revenue to support deleveraging
- Film/DTC series output roughly doubled
- Committed to 30 theatrical films/yr across Paramount + WB, 45-day window minimum

$TKO /UFC Deal Working:
- 10M+ homes have watched 100M+ hours of UFC content
- ~15x the average PPV event over past 2 years
- New UFC subs ~15 years younger than typical P+ user
- Cross-content stickiness (UFC → South Park)
- No update on NFL negotiations

Streaming Tech Stack Unification (mid-year launch):
- Converged across Paramount+, Pluto, BET+
- Pluto major upgrade this summer
- ~65% of Pluto viewing now logged-in (better ad targeting)
- Provides preview of WBD/HBO integration potential

Pro Forma (WBD + PSKY) by 2028:
- Revenue $72B
- Adj EBITDA $15.2B (incl. $4B synergies)
- Net leverage path: 6.5x at close → 5.5x → 4.7x
- FCF building to $3.7B+ by 2028

Risk/Reward:
- Bull $20 (+80%): deal closes, hits '30 targets, $20B EBITDA / $10B FCF
- Base $14 (+26%): deal closes, $16B EBITDA / $8B FCF by 2030
- Bear $8 (-28%): regulators block deal

tweets.full_text:
Morgan Stanley Research is bullish on $PSKY. They Reiterate an Overweight rating and a $14 price target (28% upside). 

Summary of the Report-

MS reiterates OW on $PSKY, $14 PT (~25% upside). Q1 EBITDA beat, FY26 guide reiterated, WBD deal on track for 3Q close. Setup is improving.

Q1 Highlights:
- Adj EBITDA $1.16B vs. MS $957M / cons $890M
- Revenue $7.3B (+2% YoY) vs. cons $7.2B
- Paramount+ subs hit 79.6M (+700K q/q)
- P+ revenue +17% YoY on January price hike + 14% ARPU growth
- DTC margins reached +10% EBITDA
- TV Media better than feared (ads -6% vs MSe -12%)

FY26 Reiterated:
- Revenue $30B (+4% YoY)
- Adj EBITDA $3.8B
- Revenue 2H-weighted, profit 1H-weighted

The Bigger Story — Standalone Synergies Build Credibility for $WBD:
- $2.5B run-rate efficiencies by YE26
- $3B+ through 2027
- Validates the $6B WBD synergy opportunity (~11% of OpEx)

WBD Deal Mechanics:
- $49B bridge successfully syndicated, to be refinanced with secured IG + HY debt before close
- Existing Paramount notes stay unsecured post-close
PIPE included a 10-year warrant per Class B share (strike floored at $12, capped at $16.02) — MS values them at ~$2-3/share
- No incremental equity issuance or asset sales assumed in base case

Strategic Signals from David Ellison:
- Flexible content licensing — flagship IP (LotR, GoT, DC, Harry Potter) stays exclusive; other content licensed to 3rd parties for high-margin revenue to support deleveraging
- Film/DTC series output roughly doubled
- Committed to 30 theatrical films/yr across Paramount + WB, 45-day window minimum

$TKO /UFC Deal Working:
- 10M+ homes have watched 100M+ hours of UFC content
- ~15x the average PPV event over past 2 years
- New UFC subs ~15 years younger than typical P+ user
- Cross-content stickiness (UFC → South Park)
- No update on NFL negotiations

Streaming Tech Stack Unification (mid-year launch):
- Converged across Paramount+, Pluto, BET+
- Pluto major upgrade this summer
- ~65% of Pluto viewing now logged-in (better ad targeting)
- Provides preview of WBD/HBO integration potential

Pro Forma (WBD + PSKY) by 2028:
- Revenue $72B
- Adj EBITDA $15.2B (incl. $4B synergies)
- Net leverage path: 6.5x at close → 5.5x → 4.7x
- FCF building to $3.7B+ by 2028

Risk/Reward:
- Bull $20 (+80%): deal closes, hits '30 targets, $20B EBITDA / $10B FCF
- Base $14 (+26%): deal closes, $16B EBITDA / $8B FCF by 2030
- Bear $8 (-28%): regulators block deal

$PSKY $WBD

BREAKING: Starship Technologies has just passed 10 MILLION autonomous deliveries.

Founded by Ahti Heinla and Janus Friis, the co-founders of Skype, this is a company that has been quietly building something extraordinary for over a decade.

The numbers are staggering:

→ 10 million deliveries completed 
→ 22 million kilometres driven autonomously 
→ 200 million road crossings: 125,000 every single day, roughly 2 per second
→ 3,000+ robots across 300+ locations in 8 countries 
→ Already $3-4 cheaper per drop than traditional rider delivery 
→ Long-term target of ~$1 per drop

That last number is the one that changes everything. According to Barclays research cited by Reuters, this shift could unlock ~$16 billion in annual profitability for delivery platforms globally.

Autonomous delivery is no longer a pilot. It is no longer a concept. It is infrastructure.

And @StarshipRobots has something no competitor can replicate overnight, regulatory approval across 8 countries built over more than a decade of real-world operations. That moat is ENORMOUS.

The robots are already on the streets of the UK, Germany, Switzerland, Sweden, Finland, Estonia and the Czech Republic. They're on 65 US university campuses. They're crossing roads twice a second.

Physical AI has arrived. And it's delivering your lunch. 🥪

~~ 

♻️ Join the weekly robotics newsletter, and never miss any news → https://t.co/GoA3ZuwoPB

tweets.text:
Barclays research shows 1 in 4 Brits are terrified their investments will go to zero. They call this an irrational "behavioural barrier" and say we must get the UK investing.

Last week in exquisite timing as the government tries to get people to invest the @bankofengland Deputy Governor, Sarah Breeden, tells the BBC: "There’s a lot of risk out there and yet asset prices are at all-time highs. We expect there will be an adjustment at some point.” [failing to mention the massive valuation disparities between the UK stock market which is lowly valued having not really kept up in with inflation in the last 30 years and the stretched valuations in the US.]

Gently chiding the Bank: it is rather difficult to build "Investment Britain" when the person in charge of financial stability is essentially telling everyone that the markets are a house of cards and  this applies to everything even though what we’re really talking about are US tech companies- reinforcing peoples’ prejudice that stock market investing is a stacked house of cards.

There are plenty of well priced UK stocks which pay dividends so when the correction inevitably comes
 you’re still getting paid to wait for shares to go back up. 

#Investing #FTSE100 #BankOfEngland #UKEconomy #Barclays

tweets.text:
【AWS的AI野心：巴克莱预测2028年AI收入将达753亿美元】

1、巴克莱银行预测，到2028年，AWS的AI相关收入将飙升至753亿美元，占其总收入的29%。这一增长主要由Anthropic和OpenAI两大合作伙伴驱动，预计届时两家公司将合计贡献超过550亿美元的收入，成为绝对的增长主力。

2、核心驱动力是AI军备竞赛下，头部模型公司对大规模算力的海量需求。AWS通过提供底层计算基础设施，深度绑定这些AI巨头，将它们的巨额研发和运营预算，直接转化为自身的高速增长。这种“卖铲子”的模式，使其成为AI浪潮中最确定的受益者之一。

3、这一预测凸显了AI工作负载正成为云服务的核心增长引擎。AWS通过与头部AI公司的战略捆绑，不仅锁定了未来数年的收入，也构建了强大的竞争壁垒。未来，云服务商的竞争格局将越来越由其服务AI生态的能力所定义。

via: Barclays Research

#AWS #AI #CloudComputing #OpenAI #Anthropic
