•$70B+ total fixed income book  ￼
•Performance:
•Met or exceeded benchmarks every year  ￼

Skillsets developed:
•Institutional fixed income (rates, credit, structured products)
•Liability-driven investing (pension mindset)
•Risk management at massive scale
•Capital allocation under constraints
•Governance + fiduciary discipline
•Early institutional research into Bitcoin’s role  ￼

2) What is that CalPERS experience actually worth in market value?

A. Replacement cost in traditional finance

Someone who:
•Managed $50B–$100B fixed income
•Has a long track record of benchmark outperformance
•Is a proven allocator (not just trader)

…would typically map to:
•Senior PM / CIO at:
•BlackRock
•PIMCO
•Bridgewater Associates

Comp range (rough):
•Base: $300K–$1M
•Bonus: $1M–$10M+
•Total comp: $2M–$15M+ annually

👉 That’s your baseline “market price” for the CalPERS skillset alone.

B. What makes his experience unusually relevant to Bitcoin treasury companies

Running a Bitcoin treasury company is NOT just “buy BTC” — it’s:

1. Capital structure engineering
•Debt vs equity vs convertibles vs preferreds
•Strive’s SATA instrument = straight out of fixed income playbook  ￼

👉 This is literally what CalPERS PMs specialize in.

2. Balance sheet optimization

Bitcoin treasury companies are:
•Levered balance sheets
•With a volatile reserve asset (BTC)

That’s structurally similar to:
•Pension funds managing long-duration liabilities with risky assets

👉 His background maps shockingly well.

3. Risk management under volatility
•Duration risk
•Convexity
•Tail events

Now translate that into:
•BTC drawdowns
•Liquidation risk
•Funding risk

👉 Again, directly transferable.

Where else do you find someone who is BOTH:
•institutional-caliber allocator
•AND orange-pilled?

This is the bottleneck. The overlap set is tiny. You’re looking for someone who has:
1.Managed tens of billions professionally
2.Understands capital markets deeply
3.Believes in Bitcoin as a primary asset
https://t.co/jTXNTQyzMQ willing to allocate corporate balance sheet to BTC

That eliminates:
•Most TradFi (they don’t believe)
•Most Bitcoiners (they’ve never managed real size)

Comparable profiles (very few)
•Michael Saylor
•Pierre Rochard (strong BTC, less large-scale PM experience)
•Select macro hedge fund guys who converted later

👉 The intersection is extremely rare.

tweets.text:
ABD Merkez Bankası (Fed), Mart ayı toplantısında politika faizini piyasa beklentilerine paralel olarak %3,50-%3,75 hedef aralığında sabit tuttuğunu açıkladı. Karar sonrası yayımlanan raporlar ve uzman değerlendirmeleri, bankanın İran ile yaşanan savaş ve yükselen petrol fiyatlarının yarattığı jeopolitik riskler karşısında piyasayı sakinleştirme eğiliminde olduğunu ortaya koydu. JPMorgan Varlık Yönetimi yetkilisi Bob Michele, Fed’in bu tutumunu piyasalara yönelik bir "endişe etmeyin" mesajı olarak nitelendirirken, bu iyimserliğin enflasyon ve iş gücü piyasası üzerindeki gerçek etkileri konusunda şüphelerini dile getirdi.

Ekonomik görünümde "kısa vadeli bir enflasyon şoku" yaşandığını belirten Michele, bu durumun büyüme potansiyelini hızlandırabileceğini ifade etse de bankanın mevcut yönlendirmesinin piyasadaki riskleri tam yansıtmadığına dikkat çekti. Öte yandan Pimco küresel ekonomi danışmanı Richard Clarida, Fed’in genel duruşunu "güvercin" ve "yapıcı" olarak tanımladı. Clarida, enerji maliyetlerindeki artışın geçici olup olmadığı konusundaki belirsizliğin sürdüğünü ve petrol vadeli işlemlerinin fiyatların zamanla düşeceğine işaret ettiğini vurguladı.
l
Kurumsal tahminlerde "iki faiz indirimi" öne çıkıyor
Küresel finans devleri, Fed’in mevcut duruşunun ardından 2026 yılı sonu için faiz projeksiyonlarını güncelledi. BBVA Research tarafından yayımlanan analiz raporunda, Federal Açık Piyasa Komitesi’nin (FOMC) karar metninde İran’daki savaşa ve bekle-gör yaklaşımına yapılan vurgu ön plana çıkarıldı. Kurum, ekonomik koşulların elvermesi durumunda 2026’nın ikinci yarısında iki faiz indirimi yapılarak politika faizinin %3,00-%3,25 terminal aralığına çekileceği öngörüsünü paylaştı.

ING analistleri de benzer bir senaryoyu destekleyerek, Fed’in Eylül ve Aralık aylarında olmak üzere bu yıl içinde iki adet 25 baz puanlık indirim gerçekleştirmesini beklediklerini açıkladı. ING’ye göre, Fed’in net bir yönlendirme yapmaması ABD dolarını doğrudan petrol fiyatlarına ve küresel risk iştahına bağımlı hale getirdi. Ancak bankanın faiz indirimine meyilli tutumu, yılın son çeyreğine doğru doların zayıflayabileceği beklentisini güçlendiriyor.

Belirsizlik kıskacında para politikası ve liderlik sorunu
Mizuho Securities stratejisti Vishnu Varathan, İran savaşı kaynaklı enerji şoklarının enflasyonu artırırken talebi baskıladığını, bu durumun Fed’i zıt riskler arasında bir yön arayışına ittiğini belirtti. Görünümün son derece düşük öngörülebilirlik taşıdığını vurgulayan Varathan, Fed Başkanı Jerome Powell’ın faiz artış olasılığını tartışmaya açtığını ancak bunun henüz temel bir senaryo olarak değerlendirilmediğini ifade etti. Bu durum, para politikasının geleceğine dair oynaklığın yüksek kalmasına neden oluyor.

Para politikasındaki belirsizliğe, Fed’in liderlik kadrosundaki değişim sancıları da eşlik ediyor. Başkan Jerome Powell’ın Mayıs ayında dolacak görev süresinin ardından durumu belirsizliğini korurken, Donald Trump’ın aday gösterdiği Kevin Warsh’ın Senato onayı Thom Tillis tarafından engellenmeye devam ediyor. JPMorgan’dan Bob Michele, Powell’ın istikrar adına ara seçimlere kadar kurumda kalabileceğini tahmin ederken, yapay zekânın ekonomi genelindeki etkileri de Fed’in uzun vadeli değerlendirmeleri arasındaki yerini koruyor.

tweets.text:
🏦 FEDERAL RESERVE — MARKET INTELLIGENCE
Usman Holdings | Macro Research
March 18, 2026
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
📌 Fed Funds Rate: 3.50%–3.75% | QT: Ended Dec 2025 | Balance Sheet: $6.6T
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔄 WHERE WE STAND

The Fed has already cut 175bps since September 2024. It now sits at 3.50%–3.75%  and has been on hold since January 2026. Two members dissented at the last meeting in favor of a cut. The debate is not whether to ease, it’s when.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
⚠️ WHY THE FED CAN’T CUT YET

→ Oil near $100 — Middle East conflict re-accelerating energy inflation
→ Core PCE still above 2% target
→ Tariff inflation still feeding through the system
→ Labour market cooling but not broken

Cutting into an oil shock is the 1970s playbook. The Fed refuses to repeat it.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
🟢 WHY CUTS ARE STILL COMING

→ Growth increasingly concentrated in AI spending — a vulnerability
→ Unemployment expected to rise toward 4.5% by year-end
→ New Fed Chair arrives May 2026 — voting roster leans 6–2 dovish
→ Political pressure from Trump for lower rates intensifying

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
📊 INSTITUTIONAL CUT TIMELINE

🏦 Goldman Sachs → September + December (2 cuts)
🏦 Barclays → September (1 cut)
🏦 CME FedWatch → December (1 cut)
🏦 PIMCO → H2 2026 (1 cut)
🏦 Hawks → Zero cuts in 2026

Consensus: Cut Sept / Dec at earliest.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
🛠️ QT IS OVER — RMP IS NOT QE

QT (Quantitative Tightening) ran from mid-2022 to December 2025 — shrinking the Fed balance sheet by $2.4 trillion from $8.9T to $6.6T.

It is now over.

What replaced it: Reserve Management Purchases (RMP) — $40B/month in short-term T-bills.

This is NOT QE. Critical distinction:

🔹 QE — buys long-term bonds to suppress rates and stimulate the economy. Emergency tool.
🔹 RMP — buys short-term T-bills to maintain bank liquidity. Plumbing, not stimulus.

QE only returns if rate cuts are exhausted AND a recession or financial crisis materializes. The US is nowhere near that threshold.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
👤 NEW FED CHAIR — KEVIN WARSH

Powell’s term expires May 15, 2026.
Trump nominated Kevin Warsh on January 30, 2026.

Warsh profile:
→ Youngest Fed governor in history (age 35 in 2006)
→ Former Morgan Stanley investment banker
→ Currently at Hoover Institution, Stanford
→ Historically hawkish — but recently shifted toward lower rates

His first FOMC meeting as chair: June 16–17, 2026.

The risk: Senate confirmation is not guaranteed. Senator Tillis (key vote) opposing until DOJ investigation into Powell is resolved. A confirmation delay creates policy leadership vacuum at the most critical monetary moment of the year.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
📅 JUNE 2026 — THE PIVOTAL MEETING

June 16–17 is where everything converges:
→ Warsh’s first meeting as chair — sets his policy tone
→ Fed cut window begins opening or closing definitively
→ Middle East situation likely at resolution or escalation point
→ Halving cycle and institutional reallocation cycles intersect

June is not just a Fed meeting. It is a macro convergence point across monetary policy, geopolitics, and markets simultaneously.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
📌 INTELLIGENCE SUMMARY

The Fed is on hold — trapped between sticky inflation and a slowing economy.
QT is over. RMP is active. QE is not on the table yet.
Cuts are coming — September to December is the window.
A new chair arrives in May. June is his first test.

When cuts finally arrive  USD weakens, real yields fall, and gold, silver, BTC and EM assets reprice simultaneously. That is the H2 2026 macro trade.

Every asset class connects back to this single pivot point.

Market intelligence only. Not financial advice. 

#Fed #FederalReserve #Macro #InterestRates #QE #QT #KevinWarsh #MacroResearch #MarketIntelligence #UsmanHoldings

tweets.text:
My view is all yield products will eventually come on-chain. 

We've already seen signs of this - it started with T-Bills, then Private Credit and now the emergence of exotic yields (e.g. USDai and DayFi)that skip TradFi rails entirely to just come on-chain. 

This is the first time in history, non-Accredited Investors can access yield products wholesale without the gatekeeping or expensive middleman fees to the bank.

As with all product categories that mature, there will be plenty of noise and users will learn that you cant simply "build your own car" even if you can. While DeFi allows DIY, a well assembled yield product requires time, tools, and technical expertise to do it safely or efficiently. 

Yuzu will be a trusted guide and yield engine for this new wave of yield instruments coming on-chain; serving everyone from the individual DeFi user to global B2C fintech platforms. As a curator, we package complex yield strategies into streamlined portfolios that actually fit the end-user. Our goal is for Yuzu to become the PIMCO of on-chain yields - a name synonymous with deep due diligence, institutional-grade research, and precise execution.

tweets.text:
Jaja
A ver porque por ahí quedó fuera de contexto. Trabaje en esos bancos pero no en WS, aquí en Arg. 
El clima en los 90 era muy similar. In 8 am out 9 pm base. Fines de semana enteros trabajando seguidos. Era inmensa la cantidad de laburo.
El idioma oficial era el inglés para todo. Escribir, hablar con clientes, sales force e incluso trading desk. 
Anécdotas muchísimas, miles.  Aprendí muchísimo, más que cualquier post grado o MBA de cualquier lado. Tenías que ser bien fuerte. La saleforce de NY y UK eran despiadados.
Mutual y pension funds venían todas las semanas. Fidelity, Templeton, Pimco, miles y miles. Eran muy jodidos y te obligaban a mantener la vara bien alta: esperaban de vos lo mismo que un equity analyst de usa UK o donde fuera. No excuses, no BS. 
El equity argentino era un asset class importante. No trabaja casi para clientes locales salvo AFJP resto todo de afuera. 
I.I. hasta hacía el ranking de los mejores equipos de research de Arg. Estaban todos MS JPM BofA SI ML RJ ….. hice incontables viajes  y roadshows a usa UK vendiendo Argentina. Tome varios cursos y labure con gente que aún mantengo el contacto y que considero personas brillantes. Tuve digamos mucha suerte de estar siempre en el lugar correcto. 
Muchas cosas me costaron mucho, entre medio de eso había que mantener a la flia (los 90s fueron trágicos para mi flia) pero no había plan B. Era avanzar, avanzar, meterle y meterle. 
Arranque empezando la facu, antes tuve algún emprendimiento propio varias historias que ahora me resultan divertidas pero que la pasé medio mal era muy pendejo. Ya en IB/ Research pase por varios y después circa 2008 me fui a la economía real medio quemado….me quedé allí cómo 12 años con algunos emprendimientos propios, muy enfocados al agro, me metí de lleno en la industria avícola, me fui a hacer un postgrado en poultry science a USA. Georgia…volví. Me hinchó las bolas laburar con animales vivos y en 2021 volví a donde arranque: Research muy feliz de estar allí de vuelta.
tweets.full_text:
Jaja
A ver porque por ahí quedó fuera de contexto. Trabaje en esos bancos pero no en WS, aquí en Arg. 
El clima en los 90 era muy similar. In 8 am out 9 pm base. Fines de semana enteros trabajando seguidos. Era inmensa la cantidad de laburo.
El idioma oficial era el inglés para todo. Escribir, hablar con clientes, sales force e incluso trading desk. 
Anécdotas muchísimas, miles.  Aprendí muchísimo, más que cualquier post grado o MBA de cualquier lado. Tenías que ser bien fuerte. La saleforce de NY y UK eran despiadados.
Mutual y pension funds venían todas las semanas. Fidelity, Templeton, Pimco, miles y miles. Eran muy jodidos y te obligaban a mantener la vara bien alta: esperaban de vos lo mismo que un equity analyst de usa UK o donde fuera. No excuses, no BS. 
El equity argentino era un asset class importante. No trabaja casi para clientes locales salvo AFJP resto todo de afuera. 
I.I. hasta hacía el ranking de los mejores equipos de research de Arg. Estaban todos MS JPM BofA SI ML RJ ….. hice incontables viajes  y roadshows a usa UK vendiendo Argentina. Tome varios cursos y labure con gente que aún mantengo el contacto y que considero personas brillantes. Tuve digamos mucha suerte de estar siempre en el lugar correcto. 
Muchas cosas me costaron mucho, entre medio de eso había que mantener a la flia (los 90s fueron trágicos para mi flia) pero no había plan B. Era avanzar, avanzar, meterle y meterle. 
Arranque empezando la facu, antes tuve algún emprendimiento propio varias historias que ahora me resultan divertidas pero que la pasé medio mal era muy pendejo. Ya en IB/ Research pase por varios y después circa 2008 me fui a la economía real medio quemado….me quedé allí cómo 12 años con algunos emprendimientos propios, muy enfocados al agro, me metí de lleno en la industria avícola, me fui a hacer un postgrado en poultry science a USA. Georgia…volví. Me hinchó las bolas laburar con animales vivos y en 2021 volví a donde arranque: Research muy feliz de estar allí de vuelta.

tweets.text:
Which signals Jeffrey get abeg.

Forex no be “signals” business. If signal dey work like that, big institutions for no dey spend billions on research, data, and infrastructure just to find tiny edge.

The truth be say real FX market na interbank market. Na banks and liquidity providers dey run am. Dem dey fight for 0.1 percent edge with PhDs, quants, risk teams, and serious systems.

Ecosystem include 
FX powerhouses.

JPMorgan, Citi, Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, Barclays, Deutsche Bank, UBS, HSBC, Bank of America.

Liquidity and market makers.

XTX Markets, Citadel Securities, Jane Street, Virtu, Jump Trading.

Asset managers wey fit run FX exposure.

BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street, Fidelity, Pimco, Franklin Templeton.

So if all these people dey hustle for tiny edge, how you wan convince me say one guy for house for Lagos get “signals” wey beat market.

Plus, Make we reason am small. If e get signal, why e dey sell am. Why e no just compound quietly.

tweets.text:
The AI trade is no longer just Nvidia, Microsoft, and Amazon.

Wall Street quietly found 15 NON-TECH stocks trading almost exactly like semiconductors.

Most investors still haven’t noticed.

2/ Renaissance Macro analyzed correlations between S&P 500 companies and the VanEck Semiconductor ETF ($SMH).

The result?

A group of industrial companies now moves almost in sync with AI chip stocks.

That’s not random.

3/ The key insight:

AI is no longer just a software story.

It’s becoming an INDUSTRIAL infrastructure boom.

And infrastructure always creates second-order winners.

4/ Here’s what the market is finally understanding:

AI models don’t run on hype.

They run on:
• electricity
• cooling systems
• turbines
• generators
• transformers
• backup power
• data-center hardware

That means industrials suddenly became AI proxies.

5/ Look at the names showing strong correlation with semis:

• Vertiv $VRT 
• Eaton $ETN 
• Caterpillar $CAT 
• GE Vernova 
• Cummins $CMI 
• Hubbell $HUBB 

None are traditional “AI stocks.”

Yet all are feeding the AI infrastructure machine.

6/ Vertiv sells cooling + power management for data centers.

Eaton sells electrical systems.

GE Vernova sells gas turbines for hyperscaler energy demand.

Caterpillar sells backup engines and generators.

The picks-and-shovels trade is expanding.

7/ This matters because hyperscalers are spending at absurd scale now.

Microsoft. $MSFT 
Amazon. $AMZN 
Meta. $META 
Google. $GOOG 
Oracle. $ORCL 

They are pouring hundreds of billions into AI infrastructure simultaneously.

Some of that capital inevitably flows into industrial supply chains.

8/ And here’s the dangerous part for investors chasing only software names:

The AI bottleneck is increasingly physical.

Not models.
Not apps.

POWER.

That’s why industrials are suddenly behaving like semiconductor stocks.

• construction cycles
• manufacturing demand
• GDP growth

Now many trade on AI capital expenditure cycles instead.

That is a massive structural shift in market behavior.

Every new frontier model requires:
• more chips
• more electricity
• more cooling
• more physical infrastructure

AI is becoming an industrial revolution disguised as a software revolution.

3/ The key insight:

tweets.text:
Financial Markets 🇺🇸 

Bitcoin surpasses $80,000 amid commentary from Pantera Capital's Cosmo Jiang. Tesla robotaxi odds rise in California; Fairlead Strategies analyzes TSLA and GOOGL technicals. Strategas Research notes market cracks; iCapital warns liquidity may fade. S&P futures hit highs, index reverses 70 points to close above 7,400; Dow ends up 0.13% at 49,768.31. Bond yields reach 4.46% session highs as stocks align

U. S. Politics and Leadership 🇺🇸 

President Trump departs for Beijing summit with Xi Jinping, first in nine years, addressing investments, Iran, Russia, tech rivalry; declares U. S.-China as superpowers. Releases amicable FDA Commissioner Marty Makary resignation message, praises accomplishments. FBI Director Kash Patel reports high morale. White House staffers admit policy sometimes stems from intuition over presidential directive. Senator Warnock critiques Trump on finances

Middle East Tensions 🇮🇱🇮🇷🇸🇦🇦🇪 

Israel deploys Iron Dome to UAE, first abroad outside U. S. Saudi Arabia launches unreported strikes on Iran. Iraq andagon: Iraq-Pakistan energy deals with Iran; Iraq leadership eyes Western investment. Hezbollah claims drone strike on Israeli vehicle in Lebanon. Trump confident Hormuz reopens soon per adviser Hassett; Pentagon eyes "Operation Heavy Hammer" if truce fails, costs near $29 billion. France's Macron proposes UN neutral mission in Hormuz

Global Diplomacy and Conflicts 🌍 

Trump entourage en route Beijing via Alaska; POTUS affirms U. S.-China superpower status. Macron in Kenya pushes Africa-Europe reset, $27 billion investments, ties to continent's fate; critiques athlete "looting." Sahel shifts to Turkiye, China, Russia alliances over France. Hamas blocks UAE-funded Rafah project contractors

Europe and Ukraine 🇪🇺🇺🇦🇳🇱 

UK's Starmer navigates internal challenges. Dutch FIDUCIAL Defense tests AI in Ukraine via Brave1; integrates with Vovkulaka drone. Ukraine's 151st Brigade destroys Russian T-90M. IRGC Tehran exercises counter U. S. invasion scenarios. Russia tests Sarmat missile

Other Developments 

Philippine senator flees to parliament amid ICC warrant over Duterte drug war. Kanye West eyes Israel tour despite Europe backlash. SpaceX gains FCC spectrum for Starlink mobile. Waymo recalls 3,800 robotaxis over flood risk. Apollo Global economist: AI replaces tasks, not jobs. Study: Friends share autism levels via brain sync. Cruise ship hantavirus probe rules out theories; 27 remain aboard MV Hondius

Trump's Beijing departure and Hormuz optimism position U. S. executive leverage to expedite maritime access, enabling direct influence on energy flows and bilateral pacts with China

Macron's Africa initiatives and Sahel realignments, alongside Iron Dome UAE deployment and energy deals, cascade into diversified alliances that reshape regional dependencies and dilute singular powers' grips on resources and security

Executive travels and defensive deployments signal pathways to stabilized trade routes, weighed against alliance shifts that fragment influence across continents

tweets.text:
【SEC推迟预测市场ETF，重演比特币基金审批拉锯战】美国证券交易委员会（SEC）近日推迟了24只预测市场交易所交易基金（ETF）的推出，表示需要更多时间研究这些产品，然后才能向投资者开放。Roundhill Investments、Bitwise和GraniteShares于2月向SEC提交了推出预测市场ETF的申请，这些基金将追踪涵盖选举、经济数据和其他现实世界事件的预测市场。根据SEC规定，ETF在提交申请后75天内自动生效，除非被SEC叫停，而75天的窗口期原定于上周到期。

ETF专家认为，尽管川普政府时期的SEC专注于放宽市场准入，但此次干预并不令人意外。Strategas Securities的ETF策略师Todd Sohn表示：“对于任何新颖的ETF投资，总会有一些最后的障碍。”他指出，预测市场ETF代表了一种新的监管挑战，因为与传统ETF不同，这些投资标的为事件合约，本质上是对现实世界事件下注，其中与政治相关的合约最受关注也最具争议。

GraniteShares首席执行官Will Rhind在一份声明中称：“我们认识到创新ETF产品通常需要额外审查，尤其是在流动性、市场结构和投资者保护方面。”他补充说，公司正与SEC保持对话，以解决任何悬而未决的问题。此次推迟让人想起现货比特币ETF获批前SEC经历的漫长审批过程，后者在2024年1月获得批准前，曾面临多年的SEC阻力，监管机构花了数月时间处理市场操纵的担忧。

SEC主席Paul Atkins在2月向美国参议院作证时强调：“投资者保护和关注市场操纵……对我和SEC来说非常重要，这已融入我们的DNA。”他指出，预测市场存在潜在的管辖权重叠问题，需要与商品期货交易委员会（CFTC）协调。CFTC对预测市场拥有主要监管权，但Atkins认为SEC需要积极参与监管这一新兴金融活动领域。

近期关于预测市场内幕交易的质疑有所加剧。律师Anthony Capozzolo指出，川普之子Donald Trump Jr.是Kalshi和Polymarket的顾问，并与一家持有Polymarket投资的公司有关联，这是此案的一个独特因素。Capozzolo表示：“至少，他们希望更好地了解这些ETF对散户投资者的潜在影响。”

ETF专家Nate Geraci认为，此次延迟反映了合理的谨慎而非敌意，与SEC处理现货比特币ETF的方式相似。他写道：“ETF包装是成熟可靠的，它有效、便捷、透明。问题更多在于它们将追踪的市场。”Geraci指出，预测市场ETF的结构性问题包括事件合约结算争议的处理，并强调即使在监管更宽松的SEC，也不是所有ETF申请都会被轻易批准。

现货比特币ETF的最终批准涉及法律诉讼和政治压力。Grayscale在2023年成功挑战了SEC，此前法院认为SEC未能解释为何区别对待现货比特币期货和比特币期货ETF。同样，Kalshi在具有里程碑意义的诉讼中胜诉，获得了推出2024年总统大选合约的权利。本周，Kalshi宣布融资10亿美元，估值达220亿美元，过去六个月机构交易量增长了800%。

Todd Sohn认为，川普政府的整体监管方法表明，SEC的暂停并非对预测市场ETF基本概念的深层反对。但他补充说，Kalshi等主要预测市场交易所相对年轻，缺乏长期的流动性测试或市场深度，这引发了一些合理问题。除非是与SEC直接沟通的发行方，否则很难知道具体的卡点是什么，而SEC是决定推迟持续时间和批准条件的最终仲裁者。

《美华快报》根据CNBC Top News综合报道。

#Polymarket #SEC #事件合约 #比特币ETF #监管 #预测市场ETF #Money #财经 #HuaMedia #华传媒

关注@ChineseAMER，发现更多精彩！

tweets.text:
AI spending is the only thing from keeping the US economy from falling off a cliff | Charles Gasparino, New York Post

The only thing keeping the US economy from falling off a cliff is AI spending: Without it, the nation’s growth numbers would be in the toilet, dragged down by an Iran war that’s goosing gasoline prices and sending US consumers straight to the poor house.

That, at least, is the doom-and-gloom making the rounds in the mainstream media and on some parts of Wall Street lately — with Cassandras adding for good measure that the artificial-intelligence bubble is poised to pop any day now, leaving millions out of a job amid a cataclysm of corporate bankruptcies.

Count me as skeptical.

Ditto for plenty of less voluble market watchers who are tracking numbers closely.

No — things aren’t perfect.

Average Americans are feeling the sting of higher prices at the pump and at the grocery store.

And yes — the AI build-out will end at some point, meaning the economy will have to perform on its own.

But take a little time to vet the facts and you might be given pause.

In fact, you might even see how things could get noticeably better and soon if a few things break our way.

Veteran market strategist Jason Trennert of Strategas Research Partners tells me that “If it weren’t for the war, we would be talking about a reacceleration in the economy.”

He points to President Trump’s tax cuts for business and his ban on tax increases for consumers.

The tax breaks “almost seem prophetic in anticipation of the war,” he said.

He also points to unemployment claims last week coming in below 200,000 — the “lowest since 1969 NOT adjusted for the rise in the population.”

Payrolls increased by 178,000 in March.

Corporate profits remain strong, which is why stocks keep setting records.

They’re also a forward-looking indicator of good stuff to come.

That’s not to say the glass-half-empty crowd don’t have their talking points.

Inflation just clocked in at 3.5%, and 3.2% when you take out volatile stuff like energy and food.

Joe Biden’s spending and Jerome Powell’s feckless monetary policy sparked a historic inflation surge that has moderated but has still left prices elevated.

Labor gets efficient

This column has never been a fan of how President Trump carpet-bombed the world with tariffs.

Treating Canada like China isn’t good policy, and while the White House has backed off the scale of the “Liberation Day” levies, they are still embedded in the US economy.

They serve both as a tax increase on consumers and at least initially, prove to be inflationary.

Meanwhile, Powell, the soon-to-be-gone Fed chair, says that while unemployment numbers remain low, for people looking for work it “doesn’t feel like a good labor market” as fewer new jobs are being created even with GDP growing.

That looks like a scary fact of life with the advent of AI.

OK — but here’s where the “panicans,” as our president likes to call them, start to lose me.

Take a look at the latest economic data: The 2% economic growth — even if it’s mostly AI driven — is a strong number that’s likely to get stronger because of AI.

Even when spending stops on the build-out of the technology, the benefits in terms of productivity begin once it takes hold and labor becomes more efficient.

Inventions create work

I know there is a bear case for AI — namely that it will spark a job apocalypse.

We’ve written about it extensively on these pages.

But that is true of every innovative technology. If history is any guide, other, better paying jobs are always created.

The invention of the car made buggy-whip makers miserable until they found work on the Ford assembly line.

BTW, another major reason for the AI build-out is Trump’s tax and regulatory agenda.

His “Big Beautiful Bill” is one of the best pro-growth pieces of policy in recent history with its major tax incentives for business investment.

The result: 10%-plus growth in this area, according to the last GDP report.

Yes, much of it comes from AI, but other sectors are likewise poised to seize the advantage once the business cycle turns.

While the Iran conflict is clearly an economic drag, it won’t last forever nor will higher gas prices.

Like the job creation from technology advancement, the same pattern follows when you get a peace dividend.

And the economy — here and abroad — is bound to pick up once investors realize that the world’s biggest state sponsors of terrorism, the mullahs in Iran, are completely neutered.

https://t.co/WXhHVZllfc

tweets.text:
Introducing Ms. Stephanie Roth: Chief Economist at Wolfe Research

In the dynamic world of economic analysis and financial forecasting, few professionals bring the blend of rigorous academic training and practical experience that Ms. Stephanie Roth does. As the Chief Economist at Wolfe Research, LLC, she has quickly established herself as a key voice in macroeconomic insights since joining the firm in November 2023. With a career spanning prestigious institutions, Roth's expertise focuses on U.S. and global economic trends, helping investors navigate complex market landscapes through data-driven models and forward-looking commentary.

Roth's professional journey is marked by significant roles that honed her skills in economic research and strategy. Prior to Wolfe Research, she served as a Senior Economist for Global Wealth Management at J.P. Morgan Private Bank. In this capacity, she was responsible for crafting U.S. macroeconomic projections that informed thousands of investment advisers worldwide, ensuring they had the tools to make informed decisions amid fluctuating economic conditions. Before that, Roth spent five years at Cornerstone Macro, collaborating with renowned economist Nancy Lazar on a top-ranked global economics team. Her work there emphasized in-depth analysis of the U.S. and Chinese economies, providing critical perspectives on international trade, growth patterns, and policy impacts.

Educationally, Roth is exceptionally well-equipped for her role. She holds a Bachelor of Science in Mathematics and Economics from Bucknell University, where she built a strong foundation in quantitative analysis and economic theory. Further advancing her expertise, she earned a Master's degree in Statistics from Columbia University, enhancing her ability to handle complex data sets and statistical modeling. This academic background underpins her approach at Wolfe Research, where she collaborates with macro analysts like Chris Senyek, Chip Miller, and Tobin Marcus to deliver comprehensive views on GDP, inflation, business cycles, and asset allocation strategies.

Beyond her technical prowess, Roth is known for her clear communication and media presence. She frequently appears in financial discussions, such as on Bloomberg Radio and CNBC's "The Exchange," where she shares insights on topics like job market cooling, soft landings for the economy, and the implications of bond market vigilantes. Operating from Wolfe's New York office, Roth continues to build her team and leverage the firm's resources to provide high-quality research that supports clients in making strategic investment choices. Her forward-thinking mindset and dedication to economic clarity make her an invaluable asset in today's uncertain financial environment.

So far in 2026, 4 stocks have delivered 50% of the NASDAQ's gains.
Can you guess which ones? 

- NVIDIA
- Microsoft
- Broadcom
- Alphabet

Per Ned Davis Research, in years where the NDX gains 10% to 25%, the median number of stocks driving half the returns is just 3.

The pattern is striking:
1987: 2 stocks
1997: 2 stocks
2024: 4 stocks
2025: 4 stocks
2026 YTD: 4 stocks

Do you preferen picking those 4 stocks or simply invest in the NDX?

I cover this every week in Panic Drop. Link in bio.

tweets.text:
Are semis in a bubble? Per Ned Davis Research, not yet.

Pre-cycle high gains:

• Semis 2000: 
+312.7% over 2 years
+903.3% over 5 years

• Semis 2025: 
+252.2% over 2 years
+469.5% over 5 years

The dot com rally delivered nearly 2x the 5 year gains we've seen this cycle.

Today's setup looks more like 1998 than 2000.

$CL_F US Dips into Oil reserves as Iran War enters third month.

This commentary was issued recently by money managers, research firms, and market newsletter writers and has been edited by Barron's.

Energy Stresses Grow

Commodities Focus NDR Ned Davis Research May 1: Total U.S. petroleum inventories, comprising gasoline, middle distillates, and jet fuel and including the SPR [Strategic Petroleum Reserve] and all refined products, are just above 1.6 billion barrels. Aggregate stocks have risen steadily over the past two years and are now up 2.1% year over year and 1% above levels at the time of the U.S. strike on Iranian nuclear facilities last June.

Despite the U.S.'s energy-independent status and no clear signs of shortages, U.S. inventory data point to broadening system stress as the [Iran] conflict moves into its third month and the effective closure of Hormuz continues.

As part of the planned 172 million barrel draw, the SPR fell 7.1 million barrels last week, the largest weekly decline since October 2022. Including commercial inventories, total U.S. petroleum stocks declined by 13.3 million barrels on the week.
$USO $XOM $SPY

tweets.text:
如果需要特定年份详细数据、板块轮动历史或最新民调影响分析，随时告诉我！美国选举年股市历史表现（标普500为主）总结历史数据显示，美国总统选举周期（4年）对股市有明显的季节性模式：中期选举年（第2年）通常是最弱、波动最大的阶段，但选举后12个月往往是反弹最强劲的时期。这些是基于1930年代至今的多项机构分析（如Ned Davis Research、Capital Group、U.S. Bank等）的统计规律，并非保证，实际表现还受经济、通胀、利率、地缘等因素影响。https://t.co/7kzMhZfqF5 +11. 总统选举4年周期平均回报（S&P 500 / Dow历史数据）中期选举年（第2年）回报最弱，第3年（选举前）最强。https://t.co/fwqWNd7GRU第1年（选举后年）：平均约9-13%（政策落地期）
第2年（中期选举年）：平均约5.8-6.2%（最弱，波动大）
第3年（选举前年）：平均约16.3-18.1%（最强）
第4年（总统选举年）：平均约6-9.5%
数据来源：Ned Davis Research、Stock Trader's Almanac（1900-2021）。https://t.co/p9f8N7jtlI +https://t.co/LEVerhIHrl（上图为1900年以来总统周期叠加趋势，显示第2年相对低迷、第3年强势。）2. 中期选举年（Midterm Year）具体表现这是2026年当前所处的阶段，历史特征非常一致：
选举前12个月：S&P 500平均回报仅0.3%-2.9%（远低于长期平均8-9%），市场常“拖累”。https://t.co/7kzMhZfqF5 +1
全年表现：平均回报约1%-5.8%，但年内最大回撤（drawdown）平均16-19%（远高于非中期年的13%）。波动率更高（标准差约16% vs 其他年13%）。https://t.co/NS9P7eoZX9 +https://t.co/LEVerhIHrl（上图为1946-2022中期选举年内最大回撤，平均约-16%至-18%。）
选举后12个月：S&P 500平均回报12.4%-16.3%（几乎是其他时期的2倍），自1950年以来每次均为正回报（18/18或更多次，无一负）。https://t.co/7kzMhZfqF5 +https://t.co/m6x1sbyztL（上图为1994-2018中期选举前后平均表现：选举前震荡偏弱，选举后明显上行。）https://t.co/hHK7GKNckl（上图为近年具体中期选举前后12个月回报对比：选举后普遍大幅反弹。）3. 总统选举年（Election Year）简要对比平均回报约11%（正回报概率约83%）。

上半年往往较弱，下半年（尤其是Q3-Q4）通常更强，选举不确定性在结果明朗后快速定价。https://t.co/p9f8N7jtlI关键结论（与2026中期选举相关）：现在（2026年4月）正处于“埋伏启动期”，历史显示波动已开始放大，年内可能出现较大回撤，但选举结果出炉后（11月后）往往迎来“解套”与强劲反弹。
市场最终更看重基本面而非党派控制——无论哪党获胜，选举不确定性消除后，股市历史表现均强劲。
过去表现不代表未来，但这一模式在过去70+年中高度一致，建议关注民调、政策预期和美联储动态，而非过度追逐短期政治新闻。

tweets.text:
Stocks have risen in recent days on hopes for an eventual peace deal between the U.S. and Iran. The S&P 500 started off the week by wiping out all of its losses since the beginning of the Iran war. On Wednesday, the Nasdaq posted its 11th consecutive day of gains — its longest win streak since Nov. 8, 2021.

“We’re basically back to where we were in late February in terms of the sentiment indicators and the valuation,” Tim Hayes, chief global investment strategist at Ned Davis Research, said on CNBC’s “Closing Bell: Overtime” on Wednesday. “If we’re going to continue higher, I think we are going to have to have a broadening out here and not be just about the Nasdaq.”

But Hayes also took on a cautionary tone, noting that investors may be too quick to jump into a market rally. $SPX

“We’re basically back to where we were in late February in terms of the sentiment indicators and the valuation,” Tim Hayes, chief global investment strategist at Ned Davis Research, said on CNBC’s “Closing Bell: Overtime” on Wednesday. “If we’re going to continue higher, I think we are going to have to have a broadening out here and not be just about the Nasdaq.”

But Hayes also took on a cautionary tone, noting that investors may be too quick to jump into a market rally. $SPX

The tech-heavy Nasdaq gained 1.59% for its 11th straight winning session, while the $SPY advanced 0.80%. However, the $DIA bucked the trend, closing slightly lower (-0.15%).

Key Drivers:
🕊️ Geopolitical Optimism: Pres. Trump suggests an Iran peace deal is "very close," with a second round of negotiations reportedly under discussion. The S&P 500 has now wiped out all "war-related" losses.

Tim Hayes (Ned Davis Research) warns of high valuations: "I don’t think this is time to jump back in... we are going to have to have a broadening out here and not be just about the Nasdaq."

👀 Watchlist for Thursday:
💰 Earnings: $PEP, $TRV, $USB, $ABT, $SCHW
📊 Macro: Weekly Jobless Claims & Industrial Production data.

tweets.text:
Thursday 09 April 2026: 
The World Clock — Worldwide https://t.co/GgjvZlg7Zf
Source: privatebank*jpmorgan*com
🔴How do geopolitical shocks impact markets?
With elections looming in more than 50 countries this year, and no signs of an end to wars in Europe and the Middle East, worries about geopolitics consistently rank as a top concern for our clients as they consider their financial plans.
What potential investment risk are you most worried about in 2024?
J.P. Morgan Private Bank client survey results
The chart describes the potential investment risk investors are most worried about in 2024 according to a J.P. Morgan Private Bank survey.
Source: J.P. Morgan Private Bank. Data as of December 31, 2023. 

But what is the connection between geopolitical concerns and market returns? It’s not always easy to discern.

In this article, we explore the nexus between geopolitical events and their market impact, analyzing over 80 years’ worth of data. We find that geopolitical events usually have no lasting impact on large cap equity returns.

However, geopolitics can have profound market impacts at the local level. We look at three examples: small cap German stocks; Hong Kong versus Singapore real estate values; and shifting dynamics in the gold market. Gold, we note, has historically been one of the best-performing tactical hedges against geopolitical risk.

In a separate and final section, we examine the growing economic and political power of the BRICS economies. The BRICS traditionally referred to Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa, but the bloc now includes Egypt, Ethiopia, Iran and the United Arab Emirates (known as BRICS+). We consider the possibility of a G7 versus BRICS+ divide and what that might mean for the U.S. dollar as the world’s global reserve currency. 
History lessons

Our tally of significant geopolitical events, shown below, begins with Germany’s invasion of France in 1940, and ends with Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
Key geopolitical events, 1940–2022
The table describes the 36 events we selected for the analysis and timing of those events.
Sources: Ned Davis Research, J.P. Morgan Private Bank. Data as of December 31, 2023.
The subsequent analysis considers equity market performance three, six and 12 months after each event, comparing the results to three, six and 12 month equity market returns during periods when there was no notable geopolitical event. We conclude that in the three months after an event the market underperforms, on average, but—this is key—six-month and 12-month subsequent returns are identical. When you consider the average equity investor experience,1 it’s as though the event never happened.
Historical data shows the typically fleeting impact of geopolitical events on equity returns
Average real S&P 500 return vs. average real S&P 500 return after geopolitical events
The bar chart describes average real S&P return (all time) vs. average real S&P return after geopolitical events. For 3-month return bars, all-time return came in at 1.3% whereas after events, return came in at 0.3%.
Sources: Robert Shiller, Haver Analytics. Data as of December 31, 2023. Note: Return refers to price return. Geopolitical events in the above chart refer to 36 events selected from 80 years of geopolitical events beginning with Germany’s invasion of France in 1940 and ending with the war in Ukraine in 2022. We measured the 3-month, 6-month and 12-month returns following these events.
Still, sometimes geopolitical events did have a lasting impact on large cap equity markets. The 1973 oil price shock, which depressed market returns over the next year, is a case in point, as the below chart reveals.2
Unlike most other geopolitical events, the 1973 oil shock did lasting damage to equity returns
Real S&P 500 return, all-time average vs. 1973 Arab oil embargo
The chart describes average real S&P 500 return vs. real S&P 500 return after the Arab Oil Embargo of 1973
Sources: Robert Shiller, Haver Analytics, J.P. Morgan Private Bank. Data as of December 31, 2023. Note: Return refers to price return. The Arab oil embargo of 1973 is one of 36 geopolitical events selected from 80 years of geopolitical events beginning with Germany’s invasion of France in 1940 and ending with the war in Ukraine in 2022. We measured the 3-month, 6-month and 12-month returns following these events.

The 1973 oil shock did have a lasting impact on equity returns. That is mainly because oil remained in short supply for an extended period, resulting in a macro state of “stagflation” (high inflation amid deteriorating productivity growth). In other words, high oil prices essentially stopped the economy from operating efficiently in the 1970s. In contrast, after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine shocked global energy markets in 2022, additional oil supply rapidly came on stream. As a result, the economic and market impact of the recent shock was less severe and sustained than its 1970s counterpart.

The main structural difference between the two episodes relates to U.S. oil production. In the 1970s, the United States was producing as much oil as it could with the technology available at the time. Thus the United States relied heavily on oil produced in the Middle East. Today, by contrast, production is flexible and U.S. oil is generally plentiful thanks in large part to the boom in shale fracking.
Unlike the 1970s, today the United States has ample supply of domestically produced oil
The chart describes oil price performance before and after the 1973 oil price shock and the 2022 oil price shock. Both lines are indexed at 100 for the time when the shock happened.
Sources: Energy Information Administration, Chicago Mercantile Exchange, Haver Analytics. Data as of June 30, 2023.
The chart describes U.S. field production of crude oil, million barrels per year
Sources: Energy Information Administration, Haver Analytics. Data as of December 31, 2023. 
Geopolitical shocks can hit local markets hard

Still, we don’t think investors can completely ignore geopolitical shocks. While the data shows that geopolitical events generally don’t have enduring impacts on large cap (globally diversified) equity markets, the story is very different at the local level, as we discuss with a few topical examples in the following section.
German small cap versus large cap companies

Global equity markets have essentially shrugged off the risks associated with the war in Ukraine (a significant geopolitical event by any definition). But the shock itself has led to a collapse in Europe’s manufacturing competitiveness, due to substantially higher energy costs (as Europe broke its link to Russian energy in the wake of the 2022 invasion). Germany has been especially hard hit.
German manufacturers have borne the brunt of higher energy costs
The chart describes Germany industry competitive position vs. non-EU countries as in net % of survey respondents saying an increase over the last three months vs. those saying a decrease over the last three months.
Sources: European Commission, Haver Analytics. Data as of March 31, 2024. Note: The data is seasonally adjusted and it refers to the net % of survey respondents saying the competitive position has increased over the last three months versus those saying a decrease over the last three months.
The chart describes U.S. industrial electricity cost vs. Germany industrial electricity cost indexed at 100 for 2020.
Sources: Bureau of Labor Statistics, Federal Statistical Office, Haver Analytics. Data as of December 31, 2023.
As a result, small cap German companies have significantly underperformed relative to their large cap peers. What’s more, the underperformance in Germany is much greater than is the small cap–large cap gap in other developed economies, as the chart below illustrates. That’s essentially because German small cap companies have been in a multi-year earnings recession—and it’s not over yet.
In part because they are more diversified globally, German large caps have outperformed their small cap peers
The chart describes the EPS 12 months forward estimates for Germany Small Cap and Germany Large Cap with January 2022 indexed at 100.
Source: Bloomberg Finance L.P. Data as of January 23, 2024. For Germany Large Cap, we used the DAX Index and for Germany Small Cap, we used the MSCI Germany Small Cap Index.
The chart shows EPS 12 months forward estimates indexed at 100 for January 2022 (small cap minus large cap) for seven countries (Germany, U.S., France, Spain, Canada, UK, Japan).
Source: Bloomberg Finance L.P. Data as of January 31, 2024. For each country, we are using the MSCI Small Cap Index and their respective benchmark large cap index. 

Meanwhile, compared with German small caps, large cap companies are more diversified globally and have a higher weighting to the technology sector and a lower weighting to energy-intensive sectors such as industrials and materials.3 As a result, large caps have better managed the challenges facing the German economy as a result of the war in Ukraine.
Hong Kong versus Singapore real estate

The changing geopolitical dynamics between China and Hong Kong in recent years have had a chilling impact on real estate values—and the economy more generally—in Hong Kong. The result: a widening gap between real estate values in Hong Kong and Singapore.

Geopolitics has shaped the market backdrop. As a result of a series of events in Hong Kong, including a 2019 protest and the enactment of the National Security Law in 2020, it is widely believed that China has tightened its grip on Hong Kong. And since 2019, observers have noted a wave of population outflows from Hong Kong to alternative locations such as Singapore, Canada and the United Kingdom.4

Hong Kong’s total population declined by 2.2% from 2019 to 2022. More dramatically, the younger part of Hong Kong’s labor force (people aged 15–29) has contracted by nearly 25% as of March 2024.

A shrinking population along with concern about the future and corporate de-risking have accelerated the downturn in the Hong Kong property market. This has led to a widening gap between real estate values in Hong Kong versus Singapore, a key destination for people leaving Hong Kong. As illustrated in the chart below, from 2019 to the present, real estate values in Singapore have risen more than 30%, but in Hong Kong they have dropped nearly 20%.
As Hong Kong’s population has shrunk, real estate values have fallen and the gap with Singapore has widened
Real estate values (residential), indexed, 100 = Q4 2019
The chart describes the real estate values (residential) for Singapore and Hong Kong indexed at 100 for Q4 2019.
Sources: Singstat, Urban Revelopment Authority, CentaData, Haver Analytics. Data as of December 31, 2023. 

To be sure, Singapore’s real estate market was always undervalued relative to Hong Kong’s, so a convergence was likely to happen in the long run. But the abrupt shift in recent years likely reflects political developments in the region.5
Shifting dynamics in the gold market

Historically, gold prices have shown an inverse relationship with real yields on U.S. Treasuries (i.e., inflation-adjusted interest rates). For example, when real yields declined, gold appreciated in value (and vice versa). As gold itself does not generate income, real yields could be seen as the opportunity cost for owning the asset. 

But changes in U.S. Treasury real rates no longer drive gold prices. The historical relationship has been largely irrelevant since 2022. The reason: geopolitics. 

The United States and its allies froze Russia’s foreign exchange reserves after the outbreak of war in Ukraine. Most of Russia’s reserves were held in U.S. dollars, and those reserves were unable to be used in a time of stress—a profound change in modern warfare. Since then, global central bank demand for gold has more than doubled as a percentage of overall demand. Central banks are diversifying toward gold, irrespective of the real interest rate backdrop, as gold is seen as “unsanctionable.”6
The long-established link between U.S. 10-year Treasury yield and gold prices has broken, while global central bank gold demand has risen significantly since 2022
The chart describes the inverted scale in % of 10-year treasury yield vs. gold price.
Sources: Wall Street Journal, Federal Reserve Board, Haver Analytics. Data as of March 31, 2024. 
The bar chart describes global central bank demand as % of total gold demand.
Sources: Metals Focus, Refinitiv GFMS, ICE Benchmark Administration, World Gold Council. Data as of December 31, 2023.

An analysis of high-frequency gold price dynamics is revealing. Although, as we’ve discussed, geopolitical shocks typically don’t have a lasting impact on global equity markets, these shocks can lead to near-term drawdowns in both stock and sovereign bond prices. Gold has typically been an effective hedge against such short-term volatility—in fact, it is one of the best tactical hedges against geopolitical risk that we are aware of.

The chart below illustrates that in the window leading up to and including a geopolitical shock, gold has typically been the best tactical hedge (although oil and the U.S. dollar have also usually worked to a lesser extent).7, 8
Gold has often proven to be a performer as a tactical hedge against geopolitical risk
Four-week return leading up to and including major geopolitical shocks (last 20 years)
The chart describes 4-week return (average & median) leading up to and including major geopolitical shocks for Gold, Oil, DXY, 10yr UST, and Stocks.
Sources: Dario Caldara and Matteo Iacoviello, J.P. Morgan Private Bank, Bloomberg Finance L.P., Haver Analytics. Data as of April 16, 2024. Note: The timeframe of the analysis goes from January 1985 to April 2024. We used the Geopolitical Risk (GPR) Index to isolate geopolitical shocks where its standard deviation is greater than 2. For consecutive series of data points exceeding 2 standard deviations, we only take into account the first data point. This analysis is based on average weekly data. DXY stands for the U.S. Dollar Index, whereas we used S&P 500 Total Return for stocks. 10yr UST stands for 10-year U.S. Treasury Bond total return. We used prices/price returns for gold, oil and DXY. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. It is not possible to invest directly in an index.​
What this means for your portfolio

As our analysis suggests, some concerns about the market impact of geopolitics are overstated. But investors cannot ignore geopolitics. While history shows that geopolitical events do not have lasting effects on globally diversified equities, the impact on local markets can be substantial.

Are your investments highly concentrated in specific markets (e.g., is your wealth concentrated in the equity you hold in a small business)? If so, you may want your assets to be more globally diversified. For investors who would like to mitigate the short-term volatility that can come from geopolitical shocks, it may make sense to add exposures to gold and oil as portfolio hedges.
What about the BRICS+?

At this juncture, we pivot to a discussion of the growing geopolitical influence of the BRICS+ economies. It’s an evolving story.9

We quantified the growing geopolitical influence of the BRICS+ vis-à-vis the G7 economies in the table below. It includes various “geopolitical power” metrics such as population size, resource production and advanced manufacturing output.
Key geopolitical power metrics for G7 and BRICS+ economies
This table describes metrics to measure collective strength of G7 and BRICS+.
Sources: World Economic Forum, World Nuclear Association, Haver Analytics, J.P. Morgan Private Bank. Data as of December 31, 2023.

Clearly, the BRICS+ are growing in strength and power—even as they lack a common currency. The U.S. dollar remains entrenched as the world’s global reserve currency, and even the BRICS+’s most powerful economy, China, has made little progress in making its currency, the RMB, more internationally held as a reserve currency.10

The chart below illustrates the dominance of the U.S. dollar across key metrics, including foreign exchange holdings by central banks and other official institutions, international lending and global currency payments.
The U.S. dollar is still the world’s dominant reserve currency, and it’s unlikely to be replaced anytime soon
% share
The chart describes each currency's % share of total when it comes to foreign exchange reserves, international debt, international loans, global payment currency (SWIFT), and foreign exchange turnover.
Sources: BIS, IMF, Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication (SWIFT) and ECB calculations. Data as of December 31, 2022. Notes: the latest data for foreign exchange reserves, international debt and international loans are for the fourth quarter of 2022. SWIFT data are for December 2022. Foreign exchange turnover data are as of April 2022.

We also examined the liabilities of the BRICS+’s multilateral development bank, the New Development Bank (NDB), which is the BRICS+ analog to the World Bank. We found that the NDB is heavily attached to the U.S. dollar: Nearly 70% of its outstanding loans are made in U.S. dollars versus just under 20% for the RMB.11

To diversify their reserve holdings, the BRICS+ economies have been heavy buyers of gold in recent years. In 2022 and 2023, more than 50% of net global official gold purchases were made by BRICS+ institutions.12 There are even anecdotal accounts that gold is being used to settle excesses in growing local currency trade between BRICS+ members.

We don’t expect the U.S. dollar to lose its dominant role in the global economy and financial system anytime soon. But there are longer-term—and unresolved—questions about the U.S. fiscal deficit (as we wrote about last fall). Clearly, too, the fact that the U.S. dollar is the global reserve currency allows U.S. citizens to live beyond the means of their country’s production capabilities.

To illustrate this phenomenon, the chart below plots current account balances versus average incomes. The assumption is that growth can be “constrained” by a country’s ability to grow its exports in line with GDP.13

As the chart underscores, the United States is an extreme outlier in the global economy. With the trendline plotted, we can calculate how much U.S. living standards (i.e., average daily incomes) would need to fall if the U.S. dollar were to hypothetically lose its reserve currency status. The decline would be catastrophic— about a 50% drop in living standards.

As we’ve said, we expect the U.S. dollar to keep its status as the world’s reserve currency for the foreseeable future. But the exercise does remind us that when a single nation’s currency dominates the global financial and trading system, imbalances may be inevitable. John Maynard Keynes warned of these imbalances when the international monetary system needed to be reconstructed in the wake of World War II.14
The dollar’s status as the global reserve currency bolsters living standards for U.S. citizens
Average daily income (USD) vs. current account balance as % of GDP
The chart describes average daily income in USD vs. current account balance as % of GDP for 27 countries.
Sources: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform (2024), IMF, Haver Analytics. Data as of December 31, 2021. Note: this data is expressed in international-$ at 2017 prices. Depending on the country and year, it relates to income measured after taxes and benefits, or to consumption, per capita. The data is adjusted for inflation and for differences in the cost of living between countries. 
We can help

Geopolitics can be a difficult subject. Your J.P. Morgan team can offer their perspective on how to evaluate the relevant risks and opportunities, as well as what portfolio changes—perhaps including added exposure to gold—might help you hedge geopolitical risk and achieve your financial goals.

1We also performed robustness tests controlling for the trend in equity prices before a geopolitical shock occurred, and the same general pattern of results held. And, while our main result is showing average returns, we also ran the analysis on median returns, which similarly didn’t change the general pattern of the results. These robustness test results are available on request.

2There are other geopolitical shocks in our dataset that resulted in a negative 12-month real return for equities; however, they tend to be correlated to the business cycle, which was likely the more important driver of returns (e.g., Russia invading Georgia in 2008) rather than the geopolitical shock itself. In our view, the 1973 oil shock is the cleanest example of a geopolitical shock doing lasting damage to equity market returns.

3For the large cap segment in Germany, the respective sector weights for technology, industrials and materials at the start of 2022 were 15.5%, 17.0% and 5.5%, respectively, whereas for small caps, the same respective sector weightings were 14.0%, 26.8% and 10.3%.

4Another factor influencing population outflows from Hong Kong is that Hong Kong experienced much harsher COVID lockdowns than other economies, including Singapore. We confirmed this via the Stringency Index produced by the University of Oxford.

5Here is a peculiarity of Singapore’s real estate: 80% of public housing isn’t actually affected by foreigners entering. Foreign buying has been concentrated at the top of the private market, namely homes above $5 million.

6This is the perception, but it is not entirely true, particularly if the gold is held away in foreign jurisdictions.

7After the peak fears surrounding a geopolitical shock fade, gold and oil tend to give back some of the gains. But in a sense, this is just making an obvious point, which is that portfolio hedging surrounding a geopolitical shock is something that needs to be actively managed.

8We even analyzed the geopolitical hedge properties of bitcoin, and while it performs decently as a tactical geopolitical hedge, the performance isn’t as strong and reliable as gold’s. Results available on request.

9Argentina recently left the BRICS, but Colombia has recently expressed interest in becoming a member of the bloc. https://t.co/82h7vLcNcW

10Furthermore, China has a mostly closed capital account; if capital can’t flow freely in and out of the country, it severely limits the ability of the RMB to function as a global reserve currency.

11The NDB is also much smaller than the World Bank in terms of its lending capacity. The NDB currently has about $33 billion of loans outstanding compared to $253 billion by the World Bank. https://t.co/kwkNzM1LWB

12Which may be an understatement, considering that Iran’s purchases are not widely monitored.

13A relationship known in the academic literature as “balance of payments constrained growth.”

14Specifically, Keynes proposed the idea of a supranational currency called the “bancor” as part of the Bretton Woods negotiations after World War II. He believed that having a global currency managed by an international authority would prevent the imbalances and economic instability that can arise when one nation’s currency serves as the world’s reserve currency. Keynes was wary of the dominance of any single currency, as it could lead to economic hegemony and power imbalances among nations. However, his proposal for the bancor was not adopted, and the U.S. dollar became the world’s primary reserve currency under the Bretton Woods system.

🔴How do geopolitical shocks impact markets?
With elections looming in more than 50 countries this year, and no signs of an end to wars in Europe and the Middle East, worries about geopolitics consistently rank as a top concern for our clients as they consider their financial plans.
What potential investment risk are you most worried about in 2024?
J.P. Morgan Private Bank client survey results
The chart describes the potential investment risk investors are most worried about in 2024 according to a J.P. Morgan Private Bank survey.
Source: J.P. Morgan Private Bank. Data as of December 31, 2023. 

But what is the connection between geopolitical concerns and market returns? It’s not always easy to discern.

In this article, we explore the nexus between geopolitical events and their market impact, analyzing over 80 years’ worth of data. We find that geopolitical events usually have no lasting impact on large cap equity returns.

However, geopolitics can have profound market impacts at the local level. We look at three examples: small cap German stocks; Hong Kong versus Singapore real estate values; and shifting dynamics in the gold market. Gold, we note, has historically been one of the best-performing tactical hedges against geopolitical risk.

In a separate and final section, we examine the growing economic and political power of the BRICS economies. The BRICS traditionally referred to Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa, but the bloc now includes Egypt, Ethiopia, Iran and the United Arab Emirates (known as BRICS+). We consider the possibility of a G7 versus BRICS+ divide and what that might mean for the U.S. dollar as the world’s global reserve currency. 
History lessons

Our tally of significant geopolitical events, shown below, begins with Germany’s invasion of France in 1940, and ends with Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
Key geopolitical events, 1940–2022
The table describes the 36 events we selected for the analysis and timing of those events.
Sources: Ned Davis Research, J.P. Morgan Private Bank. Data as of December 31, 2023.
The subsequent analysis considers equity market performance three, six and 12 months after each event, comparing the results to three, six and 12 month equity market returns during periods when there was no notable geopolitical event. We conclude that in the three months after an event the market underperforms, on average, but—this is key—six-month and 12-month subsequent returns are identical. When you consider the average equity investor experience,1 it’s as though the event never happened.
Historical data shows the typically fleeting impact of geopolitical events on equity returns
Average real S&P 500 return vs. average real S&P 500 return after geopolitical events
The bar chart describes average real S&P return (all time) vs. average real S&P return after geopolitical events. For 3-month return bars, all-time return came in at 1.3% whereas after events, return came in at 0.3%.
Sources: Robert Shiller, Haver Analytics. Data as of December 31, 2023. Note: Return refers to price return. Geopolitical events in the above chart refer to 36 events selected from 80 years of geopolitical events beginning with Germany’s invasion of France in 1940 and ending with the war in Ukraine in 2022. We measured the 3-month, 6-month and 12-month returns following these events.
Still, sometimes geopolitical events did have a lasting impact on large cap equity markets. The 1973 oil price shock, which depressed market returns over the next year, is a case in point, as the below chart reveals.2
Unlike most other geopolitical events, the 1973 oil shock did lasting damage to equity returns
Real S&P 500 return, all-time average vs. 1973 Arab oil embargo
The chart describes average real S&P 500 return vs. real S&P 500 return after the Arab Oil Embargo of 1973
Sources: Robert Shiller, Haver Analytics, J.P. Morgan Private Bank. Data as of December 31, 2023. Note: Return refers to price return. The Arab oil embargo of 1973 is one of 36 geopolitical events selected from 80 years of geopolitical events beginning with Germany’s invasion of France in 1940 and ending with the war in Ukraine in 2022. We measured the 3-month, 6-month and 12-month returns following these events.

The 1973 oil shock did have a lasting impact on equity returns. That is mainly because oil remained in short supply for an extended period, resulting in a macro state of “stagflation” (high inflation amid deteriorating productivity growth). In other words, high oil prices essentially stopped the economy from operating efficiently in the 1970s. As a result, the economic and market impact of the recent shock was less severe and sustained than its 1970s counterpart.

In the 1970s, the United States was producing as much oil as it could with the technology available at the time. Thus the United States relied heavily on oil produced in the Middle East. Today, by contrast, production is flexible and U.S. oil is generally plentiful thanks in large part to the boom in shale fracking.
Unlike the 1970s, today the United States has ample supply of domestically produced oil

Sources: Energy Information Administration, Chicago Mercantile Exchange, Haver Analytics. Data as of June 30, 2023.

Sources: Energy Information Administration, Haver Analytics. Data as of December 31, 2023.

Key geopolitical power metrics for G7 and BRICS+ economies
This table describes metrics to measure collective strength of G7 and BRICS+.
Sources: World Economic Forum, World Nuclear Association, Haver Analytics, J.P. Morgan Private Bank. Data as of December 31, 2023.

Clearly, the BRICS+ are growing in strength and power—even as they lack a common currency. The U.S. dollar remains entrenched as the world’s global reserve currency, and even the BRICS+’s most powerful economy, China, has made little progress in making its currency, the RMB, more internationally held as a reserve currency.10

The chart below illustrates the dominance of the U.S. dollar across key metrics, including foreign exchange holdings by central banks and other official institutions, international lending and global currency payments.
The U.S. dollar is still the world’s dominant reserve currency, and it’s unlikely to be replaced anytime soon
% share
The chart describes each currency's % share of total when it comes to foreign exchange reserves, international debt, international loans, global payment currency (SWIFT), and foreign exchange turnover.
Sources: BIS, IMF, Society for Worldwide Interbank Financial Telecommunication (SWIFT) and ECB calculations. Data as of December 31, 2022. Notes: the latest data for foreign exchange reserves, international debt and international loans are for the fourth quarter of 2022. SWIFT data are for December 2022. Foreign exchange turnover data are as of April 2022.

We also examined the liabilities of the BRICS+’s multilateral development bank, the New Development Bank (NDB), which is the BRICS+ analog to the World Bank. We found that the NDB is heavily attached to the U.S. dollar: Nearly 70% of its outstanding loans are made in U.S. dollars versus just under 20% for the RMB.11

To diversify their reserve holdings, the BRICS+ economies have been heavy buyers of gold in recent years. In 2022 and 2023, more than 50% of net global official gold purchases were made by BRICS+ institutions.12 There are even anecdotal accounts that gold is being used to settle excesses in growing local currency trade between BRICS+ members.

We don’t expect the U.S. dollar to lose its dominant role in the global economy and financial system anytime soon. But there are longer-term—and unresolved—questions about the U.S. fiscal deficit (as we wrote about last fall). Clearly, too, the fact that the U.S. dollar is the global reserve currency allows U.S. citizens to live beyond the means of their country’s production capabilities.

To illustrate this phenomenon, the chart below plots current account balances versus average incomes. The assumption is that growth can be “constrained” by a country’s ability to grow its exports in line with GDP.13

As the chart underscores, the United States is an extreme outlier in the global economy. With the trendline plotted, we can calculate how much U.S. living standards (i.e., average daily incomes) would need to fall if the U.S. dollar were to hypothetically lose its reserve currency status. The decline would be catastrophic— about a 50% drop in living standards.

As we’ve said, we expect the U.S. dollar to keep its status as the world’s reserve currency for the foreseeable future. But the exercise does remind us that when a single nation’s currency dominates the global financial and trading system, imbalances may be inevitable. John Maynard Keynes warned of these imbalances when the international monetary system needed to be reconstructed in the wake of World War II.14
The dollar’s status as the global reserve currency bolsters living standards for U.S. citizens
Average daily income (USD) vs. current account balance as % of GDP
The chart describes average daily income in USD vs. current account balance as % of GDP for 27 countries.
Sources: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform (2024), IMF, Haver Analytics. Data as of December 31, 2021. Note: this data is expressed in international-$ at 2017 prices. Depending on the country and year, it relates to income measured after taxes and benefits, or to consumption, per capita. The data is adjusted for inflation and for differences in the cost of living between countries. 
We can help

Geopolitics can be a difficult subject. Your J.P. Morgan team can offer their perspective on how to evaluate the relevant risks and opportunities, as well as what portfolio changes—perhaps including added exposure to gold—might help you hedge geopolitical risk and achieve your financial goals.

1We also performed robustness tests controlling for the trend in equity prices before a geopolitical shock occurred, and the same general pattern of results held. And, while our main result is showing average returns, we also ran the analysis on median returns, which similarly didn’t change the general pattern of the results. These robustness test results are available on request.

2There are other geopolitical shocks in our dataset that resulted in a negative 12-month real return for equities; however, they tend to be correlated to the business cycle, which was likely the more important driver of returns (e.g., Russia invading Georgia in 2008) rather than the geopolitical shock itself. In our view, the 1973 oil shock is the cleanest example of a geopolitical shock doing lasting damage to equity market returns.

3For the large cap segment in Germany, the respective sector weights for technology, industrials and materials at the start of 2022 were 15.5%, 17.0% and 5.5%, respectively, whereas for small caps, the same respective sector weightings were 14.0%, 26.8% and 10.3%.

4Another factor influencing population outflows from Hong Kong is that Hong Kong experienced much harsher COVID lockdowns than other economies, including Singapore. We confirmed this via the Stringency Index produced by the University of Oxford.

5Here is a peculiarity of Singapore’s real estate: 80% of public housing isn’t actually affected by foreigners entering. Foreign buying has been concentrated at the top of the private market, namely homes above $5 million.

6This is the perception, but it is not entirely true, particularly if the gold is held away in foreign jurisdictions.

7After the peak fears surrounding a geopolitical shock fade, gold and oil tend to give back some of the gains. But in a sense, this is just making an obvious point, which is that portfolio hedging surrounding a geopolitical shock is something that needs to be actively managed.

8We even analyzed the geopolitical hedge properties of bitcoin, and while it performs decently as a tactical geopolitical hedge, the performance isn’t as strong and reliable as gold’s. Results available on request.

9Argentina recently left the BRICS, but Colombia has recently expressed interest in becoming a member of the bloc. https://t.co/82h7vLcNcW

10Furthermore, China has a mostly closed capital account; if capital can’t flow freely in and out of the country, it severely limits the ability of the RMB to function as a global reserve currency.

11The NDB is also much smaller than the World Bank in terms of its lending capacity. The NDB currently has about $33 billion of loans outstanding compared to $253 billion by the World Bank. https://t.co/kwkNzM1LWB

12Which may be an understatement, considering that Iran’s purchases are not widely monitored.

13A relationship known in the academic literature as “balance of payments constrained growth.”

14Specifically, Keynes proposed the idea of a supranational currency called the “bancor” as part of the Bretton Woods negotiations after World War II. He believed that having a global currency managed by an international authority would prevent the imbalances and economic instability that can arise when one nation’s currency serves as the world’s reserve currency. Keynes was wary of the dominance of any single currency, as it could lead to economic hegemony and power imbalances among nations. However, his proposal for the bancor was not adopted, and the U.S. dollar became the world’s primary reserve currency under the Bretton Woods system.
